{"title": "EIP-3074-style Account Abstraction", "summary": "Implementation of the `AUTH` and `AUTHCALL` EVM opcodes", "description": "## Abstract\\n\\nThis AIP proposes the implementation of two new EVM opcodes: `AUTH` and `AUTHCALL`, enabling account abstraction. This is not a novel proposal; this was proposed for Ethereum in [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074), which is as of yet not implemented on Ethereum.\\n\\n## Motivation\\n\\nAccount Abstraction is the name given to a number of proposals and tools intended to extend the functionality of \\"accounts\\" in Ethereum, whether contracts or EOAs. [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) was proposed as an avenue to increase the power of EOAs (by using heavily audited and vetted entry point contracts traditionally called invokers) by enabling the ability at the protocol level for one EOA to authorize another address to spend its native asset. This unlocks a number of functionalities, and also stands to potentially enhance UX. Advanbced use cases built on this concept include session keys for games and allowing gas payment in ERC20 tokens, both without needing the standard two transaction approve-and-transfer mechanism which currently dominates the ecosystem with ERC20 tokens.\\n\\nWhile [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) has not been implemented on Ethereum as of this writing, it is the author's opinion that this does not reflect poorly on the EIP/AIP. EIP-3074 has been developed significantly, primarily by Quilt, and included repositories of patched versions of Solidity and the Geth client. It is also the author's belief that this is an excellent opportunity for Ethereum-aligned ecosystems to serve as the proving ground for proposals that can aid Ethereum.\\n\\n**_Note_:** The Specification and other sections of the AIP lean heavily on, or are outright copied from, [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074), written by Ansgar Dietrichs, Sam Wilson, lightclients, and Micah Zoltu. The authors have been contacted and have given their permission, and the work is also copyright-waived via [CC0](https://eips.ethereum.org/LICENSE).\\n\\n## Specification\\n\\n_copied from [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) with permission from the authors_\\n\\n### Conventions\\n\\n - **`top - N`** - the `N`th most recently pushed value on the EVM stack, where `top - 0` is the most recent.\\n - **`||`** - byte concatenation operator.\\n - **invalid execution** - execution that is invalid and must exit the current execution frame immediately, consuming all remaining gas (in the same way as a stack underflow or invalid jump).\\n\\n### Constants\\n\\n|     Constant     | Value  |\\n| ---------------- | ------ |\\n| `MAGIC`          | `0x04` |\\n\\n`MAGIC` is used for [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) signatures to prevent signature collisions with other signing formats.\\n\\n### Context Variables\\n\\n|     Variable        | Type      | Initial Value |\\n| ------------------- | --------- |:------------- |\\n| `authorized`        | `address` | unset         |\\n\\nThe context variable `authorized` shall indicate the active account for `AUTHCALL` instructions in the current frame of execution. If set, `authorized` shall only contain an account which has given the contract authorization to act on its behalf. An unset value shall indicate that no such account is set and that there is not yet an active account for `AUTHCALL` instructions in the current frame of execution.\\n\\nThe variable has the same scope as the program counter -- `authorized` persists throughout a single frame of execution of the contract, but is not passed through any calls (including `DELEGATECALL`). If the same contract is being executed in separate execution frames (ex. a `CALL` to self), both frames shall have independent values for `authorized`. Initially in each frame of execution, `authorized` is always unset, even if a previous execution frame for the same contract has a value.\\n\\n### `AUTH` (`0xf6`)\\n\\nA new opcode `AUTH` shall be created at `0xf6`. It shall take three stack element inputs (the last two describing a memory range), and it shall return one stack element.\\n\\n#### Input\\n\\n##### Stack\\n\\n| Stack      | Value        |\\n| ---------- | ------------ |\\n| `top - 0`  | `authority`  |\\n| `top - 1`  | `offset`     |\\n| `top - 2`  | `length`     |\\n\\n##### Memory\\n\\nThe final two stack arguments (`offset` and `length`) describe a range of memory. The format of the contents of that range is:\\n\\n - `memory[offset    : offset+1 ]` - `yParity`\\n - `memory[offset+1  : offset+33]` - `r`\\n - `memory[offset+33 : offset+65]` - `s`\\n - `memory[offset+65 : offset+97]` - `commit`\\n\\n#### Output\\n\\n##### Stack\\n\\n| Stack      | Value        |\\n| ---------- | -------------|\\n| `top - 0`  | `success`    |\\n\\n##### Memory\\n\\nMemory is not modified by this instruction.\\n\\n#### Behavior\\n\\nIf `length` is greater than 97, the extra bytes are ignored for signature verification (they still incur a gas cost as defined later). Bytes outside the range (in the event `length` is less than 97) are treated as if they had been zeroes.\\n\\n`authority` is the address of the account which generated the signature. If `EXTCODESIZE` of `authority` is not zero, consider the operation unsuccessful and unset `authorized`.\\n\\nThe arguments (`yParity`, `r`, `s`) are interpreted as an ECDSA signature on the secp256k1 curve over the message `keccak256(MAGIC || chainId || nonce || invokerAddress || commit)`, where:\\n\\n - `chainId` is the current chain's [EIP-155](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-155.md) unique identifier padded to 32 bytes.\\n - `nonce` is the signer's current nonce, left-padded to 32 bytes. Any other value is considered invalid.\\n - `invokerAddress` is the address of the contract executing `AUTH` (or the active state address in the context of `CALLCODE` or `DELEGATECALL`), left-padded with zeroes to a total of 32 bytes (ex. `0x000000000000000000000000AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA`).\\n - `commit`, one of the arguments passed into `AUTH`, is a 32-byte value that can be used to commit to specific additional validity conditions in the invoker's pre-processing logic.\\n\\nSignature validity and signer recovery are handled analogously to transaction signatures, including the stricter `s` range for preventing ECDSA malleability. Note that `yParity` is expected to be `0` or `1`.\\n\\nIf the signature is valid and the signer address is equal to `authority`, the context variable `authorized` is set to the `authority`. In particular, this is also true if `authority == tx.origin`, which used to be handled separately in earlier versions of this EIP (see Security Considerations). If the signature is instead invalid or the signer address does not equal `authority`, `authorized` is reset to an unset value.\\n\\n`AUTH` returns `1` if `authorized` is set, or `0` otherwise.\\n\\n#### Gas Cost\\n\\nThe gas cost for `AUTH` is equal to the sum of:\\n\\n - fixed fee `3100`.\\n - memory expansion gas cost (`auth_memory_expansion_fee`).\\n - `100` if `authority` is warm, `2600` if it is cold (per [EIP-2929](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-2929.md)).\\n\\nThe fixed fee is equal to the cost for the `ecrecover` precompile, plus a bit extra to cover a keccak256 hash and some additional logic.\\n\\nThe memory expansion gas cost (`auth_memory_expansion_fee`) shall be calculated in the same way as `RETURN`, where memory is expanded if the specified range is outside the current allocation.\\n\\n### `AUTHCALL` (`0xf7`)\\n\\nA new opcode `AUTHCALL` shall be created at `0xf7`. It shall take seven stack elements and return one stack element. It matches the behavior of the existing `CALL` (`0xF1`) instruction, except where noted below.\\n\\n#### Input\\n\\n| Stack     | Value        |\\n| --------- | ------------ |\\n| `top - 0` | `gas`        |\\n| `top - 1` | `addr`       |\\n| `top - 2` | `value`      |\\n| `top - 3` | `argsOffset` |\\n| `top - 4` | `argsLength` |\\n| `top - 5` | `retOffset`  |\\n| `top - 6` | `retLength`  |\\n\\n#### Output\\n\\n| Stack      | Value     |\\n| ---------- | --------- |\\n| `top - 0`  | `success` |\\n\\n#### Behavior\\n\\n`AUTHCALL` is interpreted the same as `CALL`, except for (note: this list is also the order of precedence for the logical checks):\\n\\n - If `authorized` is unset, execution is invalid (as defined above). Otherwise, the caller address for the call is set to `authorized`.\\n - The gas cost, including how much gas is available for the subcall, is specified in the Gas Cost section.\\n - If the `gas` operand is equal to `0`, the instruction will send all available gas as per [EIP-150](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-150.md).\\n - If the gas available for the subcall would be less than `gas`, execution is invalid.\\n - There is no gas stipend, even for non-zero `value`.\\n - `value` is deducted from the balance of `authorized`. If `value` is higher than the balance of `authorized`, execution is invalid.\\n\\n`AUTHCALL` must increase the call depth by one. `AUTHCALL` must not increase the call depth by two as it would if it first called into the authorized account and then into the target.\\n\\nThe return data area accessed with `RETURNDATASIZE` (`0x3d`) and `RETURNDATACOPY` (`0x3e`) must be set in the same way as the `CALL` instruction.\\n\\nImportantly, `AUTHCALL` does not reset `authorized`, but leaves it unchanged.\\n\\n#### Gas Cost\\n\\nThe gas cost for `AUTHCALL` shall be the **sum** of:\\n\\n - static gas cost (`warm_storage_read`)\\n - memory expansion gas cost (`memory_expansion_fee`)\\n - dynamic gas cost (`dynamic_gas`)\\n - gas available for execution in the subcall (`subcall_gas`)\\n\\nThe memory expansion gas cost (`memory_expansion_fee`) shall be calculated in the same way as `CALL`.\\n\\nThe dynamic gas portion (`dynamic_gas`), and the gas available for execution in the subcall (`subcall_gas`) shall be calculated as:\\n\\n```\\ndynamic_gas = 0\\n\\nif addr not in accessed_addresses:\\n    dynamic_gas += 2500         # cold_account_access - warm_storage_read\\n\\nif value > 0:\\n    dynamic_gas += 6700         # NB: Not 9000, like in `CALL`\\n    if is_empty(addr):\\n        dynamic_gas += 25000\\n\\nremaining_gas = available_gas - dynamic_gas\\nall_but_one_64th = remaining_gas - (remaining_gas // 64)\\n\\nif gas == 0:\\n    subcall_gas = all_but_one_64th\\nelif all_but_one_64th < gas:\\n    raise                       # Execution is invalid.\\nelse:\\n    subcall_gas = gas\\n```\\n\\nAs with `CALL`, the full gas cost is charged immediately, independently of actually executing the call.\\n\\n## Rationale\\n\\n_copied from [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) with permission from the authors_\\n\\n### Signature in Memory\\n\\nThe signature format (`yParity`, `r`, and `s`) is fixed, so it might seem curious that `auth` accepts a dynamic memory range. The signature is placed in memory so that `auth` can be upgraded in the future to work with contract accounts (which might use non-ECDSA signatures) and not just EOAs.\\n\\n### Signing Address `auth` Argument\\n\\nIncluding `authority` (the signing address) as an argument to `auth` allows future upgrades to the instruction to work with contract accounts, and not just EOAs.\\n\\nIf `authority` were not included and multiple signature schemes allowed, it would not be possible to compute the authorizing account's address from just the signature alone.\\n\\n### Reserving One Sixty-Fourth of Available Gas\\n\\n`AUTHCALL` will not pass more than 63/64th of the available gas for the reasons enumerated in [EIP-150](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-150.md).\\n\\n### Throwing for Unset `authorized` During `AUTHCALL`\\n\\nA well-behaved contract should never reach an `AUTHCALL` without having successfully set `authorized` beforehand. The safest behavior, therefore, is to exit the current frame of execution immediately. This is especially important in the context of transaction sponsoring / relaying, which is expected to be one of the main use cases for this EIP. In a sponsored transaction, the inability to distinguish between a sponsee-attributable fault (like a failing sub-call) and a sponsor-attributable fault (like a failing `AUTH`) is especially dangerous and should be prevented because it charges unfair fees to the sponsee.\\n\\n### Another Sponsored Transaction EIP\\n\\nThere are two general approaches to separating the \\"fee payer\\" from the \\"action originator\\".\\n\\nThe first is introducing a new transaction type. This requires significant changes to clients to support and is generally less upgradeable than other solutions (e.g. this EIP). This approach is also not immediately compatible with account abstraction (AA). These proposals require a *signed* transaction from the sponsor's account, which is not possible from an AA contract, because it has no private key to sign with. The main advantage of new transaction types is that the validity requirements are enforced by the protocol, therefore invalid transactions do not antlute block space.\\n\\nThe other main approach is to introduce a new mechanism in the EVM to masquerade as other accounts. This EIP introduces `AUTH` and `AUTHCALL` to make calls as EOAs. There are many different permutations of this mechanism. An alternative mechanism would be to add an opcode that can make arbitrary calls based on a similar address creation scheme as `CREATE2`. Although this mechanism would not benefit users today, it would immediately allow for those accounts to send and receive ether -- making it feel like a more first-class primitive.\\n\\nBesides better compatibility with AA, introducing a new mechanism into the EVM is a much less intrusive change than a new transaction type. This approach requires no changes in existing wallets, and little change in other tooling.\\n\\n`AUTHCALL`'s single deviation from `CALL` is to set `CALLER`. It implements the minimal functionality to enable sender abstraction for sponsored transactions. This single mindedness makes `AUTHCALL` significantly more composable with existing Ethereum features.\\n\\nMore logic can be implemented around the `AUTHCALL` instruction, giving more control to invokers and sponsors without sacrificing security or user experience for sponsees.\\n\\n### What to Sign?\\n\\nAs originally written, this proposal specified a precompile with storage to track nonces. Since a precompile with storage is unprecedented, a revision moved replay protection into the invoker contract, necessitating a certain level of user trust in the invoker. Expanding on this idea of trusted invokers, the other signed fields were eventually eliminated, one by one, until only `invoker` and `commit` remained. To appease concerns about cross-chain replay attacks and irrevocable signatures, the `chainId` and `nonce` fields returned to the signed message.\\n\\nThe `invoker` binds a particular signed message to a single invoker. If invoker was not part of the message, any invoker could reuse the signature to completely compromise the EOA. This allows users to trust that their message will be validated as they expect, particularly the values committed to in `commit`.\\n\\n### Understanding `commit`\\n\\nEarlier iterations of this EIP included mechanisms for replay protection, and also signed over value, gas, and other arguments to `AUTHCALL`. After further investigation, we revised this EIP to its current state: explicitly delegate these responsibilities to the invoker contract.\\n\\nA user will specifically interact with an invoker they trust. Because they trust this contract to execute faithfully, they will \\"commit\\" to certain properties of a call they would like to make by computing a hash of the call values. They can be certain that the invoker will only allow the call to proceed if it is able to verify the values committed to (e.g. a nonce to protect against replay attacks). This certainty arises from the `commit` value that is signed over by the user. This is the hash of values which the invoker will validate. A safe invoker should accept the values from the user and compute the commit hash itself. This ensures that invoker operated on the same input that user authorized.\\n\\n![auth message format](../assets/AIP-22/auth-msg.png)\\n\\nUsing `commit` as a hash of values allows for invokers to implement arbitrary constraints. For example, they could allow accounts to have `N` parallel nonces. Or, they could allow a user to commit to multiple calls with a single signature. This would allow multi-tx flows, such as [ERC-20](https://github.com/ethereum/ercs/blob/master/ERCS/erc-20.md) `approve`-`transfer` actions, to be condensed into a single transaction with a single signature verification. A commitment to multiple calls would look something like the diagram below.\\n\\n![multi-call auth message](../assets/AIP-22/auth-msg-multi-call.png)\\n\\nAnother interesting use is to delegate control of the EOA to other key(s). This would mean the EOA signs a `commit` message with the address of the key(s) and an access anticy, if applicable. When the delegate wants to make a call as the EOA it will construct a signature over the invoker-specified call format and relay it (with the actual call data) onto chain with the signature and commit that granted it access to the account. The invoker will then be able to determine that the EOA has allowed this alternative key to make calls on its behalf.\\n\\n![delegate auth message](../assets/AIP-22/auth-msg-delegate.png)\\n\\n\\n### Invoker Contracts\\n\\nThe invoker contract is a trustless intermediary between the sponsor and sponsee. A sponsee signs over `invoker` to require the transaction to be processed only by a contract they trust. This allows them to interact with sponsors without needing to trust them.\\n\\nChoosing an invoker is similar to choosing a smart contract wallet implementation. It's important to choose one that has been thoroughly reviewed, tested, and accepted by the community as secure. We expect a few invoker designs to be utilized by most major transaction relay providers, with a few outliers that offer more novel mechanisms.\\n\\nAn important note is that invoker contracts **MUST NOT** be upgradeable. If an invoker can be redeployed to the same address with different code, it would be possible to redeploy the invoker with code that does not properly verify `commit` and any account that signed a message over that invoker would be compromised. Although this sounds scary, it is no different than using a smart contract wallet via `DELEGATECALL`. If the wallet is redeployed with different logic, all wallets using its code could be compromised.\\n\\n### On Call Depth\\n\\nThe EVM limits the maximum number of nested calls, and naively allowing a sponsor to manipulate the call depth before reaching the invoker would introduce a griefing attack against the sponsee. That said, with the 63/64th gas rule, and the cost of `AUTHCALL`, the stack is effectively limited to a much smaller depth than the hard maximum by the `gas` parameter.\\n\\nIt is, therefore, sufficient for the invoker to guarantee a minimum amount of gas, because there is no way to reach the hard maximum call depth with any reasonable (i.e. less than billions) amount of gas.\\n\\n### Source of `value`\\n\\nIn previous iterations of this EIP, it was thought that deducting value from an EOA mid-execution was probledao. This was due to an invariant of pending transactions which allows tx pools to statically determine the validity of a given transaction.\\n\\nHowever, after further investigation we found that breaking the invariant is safe. This is mostly due to the fact that the worst case is similar in both instances.\\n\\nCurrently an attacker can queue many transactions in the tx pool, across many accounts, and invalidate them all at once with a block where each of the queued accounts send a tx moving their entire balance. This attack will become easier and cheaper after this EIP, because it will no longer require direct access to the block builder and will not cost a full 21000 gas to originate each tx. However, the attack does not have a substantial impact on the network, so reducing the difficulty and cost is not of concern.\\n\\n### Allowing `tx.origin` as Signer\\n\\nAllowing `authorized` to equal `tx.origin` enables simple transaction batching, where the sender of the outer transaction would be the signing account. The ERC-20 approve-then-transfer pattern, which currently requires two separate transactions, could be completed in a single transaction with this proposal.\\n\\n`AUTH` allows for signatures to be signed by `tx.origin`. For any such signatures, subsequent `AUTHCALL`s have `msg.sender == tx.origin` in their first layer of execution. Without EIP-3074, this situation can only ever arise in the topmost execution layer of a transaction. This EIP breaks that invariant and so affects smart contracts containing `require(msg.sender == tx.origin)` checks. This check can be used for at least three purposes:\\n\\n 1. Ensuring that `msg.sender` is an EOA (given that `tx.origin` always has to be an EOA). This invariant does not depend on the execution layer depth and, therefore, is not affected.\\n 2. Protecting against atomic sandwich attacks like flash loans, that rely on the ability to modify state before and after the execution of the target contract as part of the same atomic transaction. This protection would be broken by this EIP. However, relying on `tx.origin` in this way is considered bad practice, and can already be circumvented by miners conditionally including transactions in a block.\\n 3. Preventing reentrancy.\\n\\nExamples of (1) and (2) can be found in contracts deployed on Ethereum mainnet, with (1) being more common (and unaffected by this proposal.) On the other hand, use case (3) is more severely affected by this proposal, but the authors of this EIP did not find any examples of this form of reentrancy protection, though the search was non-exhaustive.\\n\\nThis distribution of occurrences—many (1), some (2), and no (3)—is exactly what the authors of this EIP expect, because:\\n\\n - Determining if `msg.sender` is an EOA without `tx.origin` is difficult (if not impossible.)\\n - The only execution context which is safe from atomic sandwich attacks is the topmost context, and `tx.origin == msg.sender` is the only way to detect that context.\\n - In contrast, there are many direct and flexible ways of preventing reentrancy (ex. using a storage variable.) Since `msg.sender == tx.origin` is only true in the topmost context, it would make an obscure tool for preventing reentrancy, rather than other more common approaches.\\n\\nThere are other approaches to mitigate this restriction which do not break the invariant:\\n\\n - Set `tx.origin` to a constant `ENTRY_POINT` address for `AUTHCALL`s.\\n - Set `tx.origin` to the invoker address for `AUTHCALL`s.\\n - Set `tx.origin` to a special address derived from any of the sender, invoker, and/or signer addresses.\\n - Disallow `authorized == tx.origin`. This would make the simple batching use cases impossible, but could be relaxed in the future.\\n\\n### `AUTHCALL` cheaper than `CALL` when sending value\\n\\nSending non-zero value with `CALL` increases its cost by 9,000. Of that, 6,700 covers the increased overhead of the balance transfer and 2,300 is used as a stipend into the subcall to seed its gas counter. `AUTHCALL` does not provide a stipend and thus only charges the base 6,700.\\n\\n### In-Protocol Revocation\\n\\nThis EIP has gone [back and forth](#what-to-sign) on how to deal with `AUTH` message revocation. Without revocation, this EIP is a supremely powerful and flexible primitive for developers. However, it does have risk for users who use insecure and/or actively malicious invokers.\\n\\nMuch of the risk is due to the new ability for users to batch many operations in a single transaction. It becomes easier for an account to be drained. This is a risk that will continue to grow, regardless of the adoption of this EIP, due to overwhelming desire for the feature and attempts to support it at the protocol level and at the app level.\\n\\nA new class of risk is introduced for insecure and buggy invokers. If an invoker has implemented replay protection, as per the authors' recommendation, this should substantially contain the blast radius. However, if the bug allows an adversary to circumvent the replay protection mechanism, it may give them full access to any EOA which has interacted with the vulnerable invoker.\\n\\nAlthough this is a truly catastrophic event which is not expected to be possible via reputable wallets, it is a serious consideration. Without in-protocol revocation, users have no way to remove their account from the vulnerable invoker.\\n\\nFor this reason, `AUTH` requires the `nonce` in the message to be equal to the signer's current nonce. This way, a single tx from the EOA will cause the nonce to increase, invalidating all outstanding authorizations.\\n\\n### Failing on `EXTCODESIZE` check\\n\\nIn [EIP-3607](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-3607.md), it was determined that the protocol should reject any transaction which originates from an account with code. Although this EIP focused on transaction origination, the authors of EIP-3074 feel the intention is clear: an account that has both code and a known private key should not be allowed to make arbitrary calls on behalf of said account. Therefore, the property is upheld in this EIP. For full rationale, please refer to [EIP-3607](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-3607.md).\\n\\n## Backwards Compatibility\\n\\nAlthough this EIP poses no issues for backwards compatibility, there are concerns that it limits future changes to accounts by further enshrining ECDSA signatures. For example, it might be desirable to eradicate the concept of EOAs altogether, and replace them with smart contract wallets that emulate the same behavior. This is fully compatible with the EIP as written, however, it gets tricky if users can then elect to \\"upgrade\\" their smart contract wallets to use other methods of authentication -- e.g. convert into a multi-sig. Without any changes, `AUTH` would not respect this new logic and continue allowing the old private key to perform actions on behalf of the account.\\n\\nA solution to this would be at the same time that EOAs are removed, to modify the logic of `AUTH` to actually call into the account with some standard message and allow the account to determine if the signature / witness is valid. Further research should be done to understand how invokers would need to change in this situation and how best to write them in a future-compatible manner.\\n\\n## Reference Implementation\\n\\n* https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/pull/28615, Geth (from original EIP authors)\\n\\n* https://github.com/test/bor/pull/1226, Bor\\n\\nThis is a cherry-picked version of the original geth implementation (of which bor is a fork). It is still under testing and should be viewed as a work in progress.\\n\\n* https://github.com/Mrtenz/transaction-invoker, Mrtenz\\n\\nA sample invoker contract along with a patched version of Solidity. This version of Solidity also follows the outdated version of the EIP.\\n\\n* https://github.com/0xAragon/account-abstraction-invoker, [Zero Ekkusu](https://github.com/ZeroEkkusu)\\n\\nReference for work on invokers for Aragon's Edge project based on Mrtenz's invoker, linked above. (3074 was not ultimately included in the Edge project, however.)\\n\\n## Security Considerations\\n\\n_copied from [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) with permission from the authors_\\n\\n### Secure Invokers\\n\\nThe following is a non-exhaustive list of checks/pitfalls/conditions that invokers *should* be wary of:\\n\\n - Replay protection (ex. a nonce) should be implemented by the invoker, and included in `commit`. Without it, a malicious actor can reuse a signature, repeating its effects.\\n - `value` should be included in `commit`. Without it, a malicious sponsor could cause unexpected effects in the callee.\\n - `gas` should be included in `commit`. Without it, a malicious sponsor could cause the callee to run out of gas and fail, griefing the sponsee.\\n - `addr` and `calldata` should be included in `commit`. Without them, a malicious actor may call arbitrary functions in arbitrary contracts.\\n\\nA poorly implemented invoker can *allow a malicious actor to take near complete control over a signer's EOA*.\\n\\n### Allowing `tx.origin` as Signer\\n\\nAllowing `authorized` to equal `tx.origin` has the possibility to:\\n\\n - Break atomic sandwich protections which rely on `tx.origin`;\\n - Break reentrancy guards of the style `require(tx.origin == msg.sender)`.\\n\\nThe authors of this EIP believe the risks of allowing `authorized` to equal `tx.origin` are acceptable for the reasons outlined in the Rationale section.\\n\\n### Sponsored Transaction Relayers\\n\\nIt is possible for the `authorized` account to cause sponsored transaction relayers to spend gas without being reimbursed by either invalidating the authorization (i.e. increasing the account's nonce) or by sweeping the relevant assets out of the account. Relayers should be designed with these cases in mind, possibly by requiring a bond to be deposited or by implementing a reputation system.\\n\\n## Copyright\\n\\nAll copyrights and related rights in this work are waived under [CC0 1.0 Universal](https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/legalcode).\\n", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Forum\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://forum.aragon.technology/t/aip-add-eip-3074-style-account-abstraction/12959/1\\"},{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/xavikh/fantastic-aip/blob/main/AIPs/AIP-22.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "Aragon Protocol Council", "summary": "Proposes a Protocol Council for Aragon 2.0", "description": "## Abstract\\n\\nThis proposal introduces the Protocol Council governance body responsible for performing regular and emergency upgrades to system smart contracts, i.e. components of Aragon protocols implemented in the form of smart contracts on Ethereum.\\n\\n## Motivation\\n\\nA need for security, as well as for the ability to seamlessly move to an improved version under specific circumstances, dictates the requirement for upgradeability as it relates to core pieces of Aragon 2.0 architecture implemented as L1 smart contracts. \\n\\nThe Protocol Council – 13 publicly-named members jointly acting to execute changes on Aragon infrastructure – answers the need for security and upgradeability. \\n\\nAs representatives of the community, the Protocol Council's main objective is to perform regular and emergency changes with varying timelocks and internal consensus requirements to the Aragon 2.0 components. \\n\\nThe Protocol Council is an initial step towards the final result of Aragon 2.0 governance – an on-chain, trust-minimized, and community-based framework for efficient and decentralized decision-making, which will be formalized in future proposals. \\n\\n## Specification\\n\\n### Protocol Council Contracts\\nIt is proposed that the Protocol Council be composed of four separate [Gnosis Safe](https://github.com/safe-global/safe-contracts) contracts.\\n\\n- an Ethereum mainnet contract with 7/13 consensus requirement (0x9A53B1651C8Dd587c30392f8931e61daBBB50899)\\n- an Ethereum mainnet contract with 10/13 consensus requirement (0x37D085ca4a24f6b29214204E8A8666f12cf19516)\\n- a Aragon PoS contract with 7/13 consensus requirement\\n- a Aragon PoS contract with 10/13 consensus requirement\\n\\nTimelocks are configured relative to the consensus requirements and are described in detail below.\\n\\n### Ownership\\n\\nIt is proposed that the Protocol Council will represent the community in governing all future Aragon 2.0 system smart contracts.\\n\\nInitially, the Council will have the ability to make emergency and regular track changes to the following contracts described in [AIP-17](https://github.com/xavikh/fantastic-aip/blob/main/AIPs/AIP-17.md):\\n\\n- [ANT Token Contract](https://github.com/xavikh/fantastic-aip/blob/main/AIPs/AIP-17.md#ant-token-contract) (*Note: the contract is nonupgradable. 10/13 Emergency route can enable/disable Permit2 approvals*)\\n- [ANT Migration Contract](https://github.com/xavikh/fantastic-aip/blob/main/AIPs/AIP-17.md#migration-contract)\\n- [Emission Manager Contract](https://github.com/xavikh/fantastic-aip/blob/main/AIPs/AIP-17.md#emission-manager-contract)\\n\\n### Timelock \\n\\nThe above contracts will be changeable by use of one of the two routes:  \\n  \\n-   a regular change route requiring 7/13 consensus of the Protocol Council with a 10-day timelock (0x29A6f32f36EDeD399763524018F17F03B1435b18), which will be used for all configuration changes in the contract;\\n-   an emergency change route requiring 10/13 consensus of the Protocol Council with no timelock, which will be used to upgrade contract implementations in the proxy contracts.\\n\\n### Proposed Signers: \\n\\n-   Jordi Baylina (Aragon Labs)\\n-   Viktor Bunin (Coinbase)\\n-   Mariano Conti (independent)\\n-   Justin Drake (Ethereum Foundation)\\n-   Gauntlet\\n-   Mudit Gupta (Aragon Labs)\\n-   L2Beat\\n-   Zaki Manian (Sommelier Finance)\\n-   Anthony Sassano (The Daily Gwei)\\n-   Liz Steininger (Least Authority)\\n-   Jerome de Tychey (EthCC)\\n-   Mehdi Zerouali (Sigma Prime)\\n-   ZachXBT (independent)\\n\\n## Rationale\\n\\n### Principles for Member Selection\\n\\nThe proposed Protocol Council list of members comprises thirteen community representatives of which the selection process has been guided by factors listed below. \\n\\n#### Ecosystem Reputation\\n\\nMembers of the Protocol Council should be value-aligned with Ethereum, the L2 ecosystem, and the wider Web3 ethos, having historically proven themselves as engaged and committed members of the relevant communities. \\n  \\n#### Resilient Nature of the Protocol Council \\n\\nThe composition of the Protocol Council should ensure (operational) resilience, including by means of jurisdictional diversity, organizational diversity, and identification diversity, e.g., including public persons, anonymous individuals, and companies.\\n\\n#### Self-limitation of Influence by Aragon Labs \\n\\nNo entity should hold wide sway over the decision-making of the Protocol Council, and this is mainly expressed in the list of proposed decision-makers, including limiting Aragon Labs’ representation on the Council.\\n\\n#### Technical and Governance Ability\\n\\nProtocol Council members should demonstrate a level of technical, security, and governance competence, necessary for performing their duties and relevant to ensuring they’re acting in the community's best interest. This competence can be quantified by looking at member’s past participation in Web3 governance frameworks, security Councils, and technical involvement in the L2 space, among other experiences.\\n\\nThe Protocol Council will be able to add or remove members and otherwise determine its affairs among the members, governed by the AIP framework.\\n\\n### Principles for Protocol Council Architecture\\n\\nIn order to optimize for both security and efficiency, a dual-route approach is introduced.\\n\\nRegular, i.e., non-emergency, changes to the contracts are facilitated by a 7/13 (55%) consensus to maximize efficiency. These types of changes require a timelock delay of 10 days to ensure the ability for the community to exit the system before any change takes place. \\n\\nAt the same time, an emergency route can facilitate immediate changes to system smart contracts in case of a critical issue. A timelock mechanism proves inefficient in such scenarios,  as it’s unable to ensure any potential issue remains contained and is addressed immediately without opportunities for outside interference, due to the public nature of timelocked transactions. Consequently, an emergency route for changing system smart contracts is introduced, requiring a very high 10/13 consensus of the Protocol Council to execute any change without a timelock. The security of this approach comes from a two-fold consideration:\\n\\n-   What’s the flat number of compromised actors necessary to perform a malicious change? (10)\\n-   Of the overall actor set, what’s the percentage of compromised actors necessary to perform a malicious change? (77%)\\n\\nAs a result, any immediately-executable malicious change would require a large number (10) of  hypothetically-compromised actors, who at the same time make up the overwhelming majority (77%) of the Protocol Council set, leading to the conclusion that a successful attack is improbable.\\n\\nWhile in the initial implementation ANT contract changes will be governed by either one of the execution routes (regular or emergency), a future AIP will introduce dual route execution allowing the regular change route to be the main execution route, with the emergency route being enabled for a select number of system contract changes. \\n\\n## Backward Compatibility\\n\\nThis change causes no identifiable backward incompatibilities. \\n\\n## Security Considerations\\n\\nAragon 2.0 is a significant technical upgrade of the Aragon system with broadscale changes to the PoS network, as well as the introduction of several novel systems. There are inherent risks in this migration, however, each component will undergo extensive testing, auditing, and public scrutiny prior to activation. The staging of the AIPs and the methodical, piecemeal upgrade of the system are intended to ensure there is sufficient time for testing, auditing, and scrutiny. \\n\\nDuring the setup of the `TIMELOCK`, and as part of the default parameters, the address `0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000001` is assigned to the `TIMELOCK_ADMIN_ROLE`. This address does not introduce any security risks, however for system hygiene reasons it is proposed to be removed.\\n\\n## Copyright\\n\\nAll copyrights and related rights in this work are waived under CC0 1.0 Universal.\\n", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Forum\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://forum.aragon.technology/t/aip-29-aragon-protocol-council/13075\\"},{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/xavikh/fantastic-aip/blob/main/AIPs/AIP-29.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "Increase Max Code Size Limit to 32KB", "summary": "Proposal to increase Max Code Size Limit from 24KB to 32KB", "description": "## Abstract\\n\\nThis AIP proposes increasing the `MAX_CODE_SIZE` Limit set in [EIP-170](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-170.md) from `0x6000 (2**14+2**13)` to `0x8000 (2**15)`.\\n\\n### Motivation\\n\\nThe author proposes raising the limit to `0x8000` to allow for the deployment of more complex contracts without needing to resort to alternative development patterns.\\n  \\n### Specification\\n\\nAt block `FORK_BLKNUM`, the `MAX_CODE_SIZE` limit changes from `0x6000` bytes to `0x8000` bytes. If contract creation initialization returns data with length of more than `0x8000` bytes, contract creation fails with an out of gas error.\\n\\n### Backward Compatibility\\n\\nThis change causes no identifiable backward incompatibilities.  \\n\\n### Security Considerations \\n\\nAdded in the Spurious Dragon Ethereum Hard Fork, `MAX_CODE_SIZE` was introduced to cap an attack vector where a quadratic action, O(n) of reading code from disk, was charged a constant gas. At the time the limit `0x6000` was set as there were no contracts of that size. Since that time, contracts have gotten more complex and frequently brush up against that limit, leading to the need to use various different methods of reducing contract size, some of which can introduce vulnerabilities.\\n\\nStorage mediums have gotten faster since this limit was introduced so fetching large data is easier. Furthermore, 32KB fits perfectly in two 16 KB I/O blocks or a single 32 KB I/O block.\\n\\n### References\\n\\n-   [EIP-170: Contract code size limit](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-170.md)\\n  \\n### Copyright\\n\\nAll copyrights and related rights in this work are waived under CC0 1.0 Universal.  \\n", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Forum\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://forum.aragon.technology/t/aip-30-increase-max-code-size-limit-to-32kb/13266\\"},{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/xavikh/fantastic-aip/blob/main/AIPs/AIP-30.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "Increase StateSync Confirmations", "summary": "Proposal to increase the statesync confirmations in bor", "description": "### Abstract \\n\\nAt block #39599624, The Polygon PoS chain experienced a reorg with a depth of 157 blocks. It was observed that the cause of this was due to two forks running with more than 16 blocks of depth, which is greater than one sprint length. As a result of this, the value of to (which is used to fetch the state sync events and calculated based on sprint length) was different on both forks, causing a bad block error to occur.\\n\\nFollowing the bad block error, reorgs were occurring at a greater frequency than normal and block propagation was affected as peers would reject blocks and fail to broadcast them further. \\n\\nThis caused issues with the user experience and chain stability which we seek to address with this proposed solution.\\n\\n### Rationale\\n\\nAt the start of each sprint, when Bor commits the state (which includes state sync transactions), it fetches state sync events from Heimdall using the two arguments below:\\n\\n\\n1. `fromID` - value which is a unique, incremental identity of the state, and;\\n2. `to` - value, which is a timestamp. \\n\\nUsing these two arguments Bor is directed to fetch from Heimdall all the state sync events that occurred between `fromID` and `to`.\\n\\nThe calculation of `to` occurs in Bor and depends on the sprint length, to is the timestamp of the block (`n`) which is a ‘sprint length’ behind the ‘current block’:\\n\\n- ```\\n n = (current block number) - (sprint length)\\n ```\\n\\nOnce the block number of `n` is calculated, Bor fetches the block from its local database.\\n\\nThe issue can occur during network partitions when two forks are running in parallel. During a partition, there is a high chance that the timestamp of block `n` will differ between parallel forks. As the forks would be running in isolation, the block formation would not happen simultaneously, causing unequal timestamps, and by extension, values for `to`. \\n\\nThe Delhi Hardfork reduced the sprint length from 64 to 16 blocks. As a result of this, Bor now fetches state sync events from Heimdall more frequently as it occurs at the start of each new sprint. The result of this is that Bor nodes running different forks could receive a different number of state sync events (due to the calculation ‘to’) from Heimdall during shallower partitions that tend to occur more frequently.\\n\\n#### Example 1. \\n\\nAssuming that we have two different forks, “A” and “B”, with a different number of state sync transactions at the start of a particular sprint:\\n\\nIf there are 2 state syncs in fork A and 3 state syncs in fork B, when forks A and B merge back, let’s say fork B merges back to fork A, then B will import A’s state. \\n\\nWhen nodes on fork B import the state from fork A, Heimdall will attempt to fetch the state sync from Bor. At this point (as mentioned above) Bor will calculate the block as `n = (current block number) - (sprint length)`. \\n\\nWhen nodes from fork B fetch block `n` from the local database, the calculation will be based on the state of fork B and not the one from the incoming chain. This causes the value of `to` to differentiate between the two forks and Heimdall will return 3 state syncs instead of 2. This will result in a `BAD BLOCK` error (invalid merkle root). \\n\\n### Specification\\n\\nThis PIP proposes to Introduce a new genesis parameter called `stateSyncConfirmationMultiplyer`. This will be used in conjunction with the current calculation to increase the block range used to calculate the `to` timestamp while fetching the state-sync events from Heimdall. \\n\\n#### Genesis File\\nWe will introduce a new genesis parameter `stateSyncConfirmationMultiplyer` which will be stored as a `map[string]unit64` and used to calculate the block interval for state-sync confirmations.\\n```\\n\\"stateSyncConfirmationMultiplyer\\": { \\n\\t\\"0\\": 1, \\n\\t\\"HF_BLOCK\\": 8 \\n},\\n```\\n\\n#### Bor Consensus Rules\\n\\nCurrent Implementation:\\n```\\n// Currently sprintLength is 16\\nto = GetHeader(currentBlockNumber - sprintLength).Time\\n```\\n\\nProposed Change:\\n```\\n// fetch stateSyncConfirmationMultiplyer from genesis file\\nstateSyncConfirmationMultiplyer := FetchStateSyncMultiplyer(currentBlockNumber)\\n\\nto = GetHeader(currentBlockNumber - (sprintLength*stateSyncConfirmationMultiplyer)).Time\\n```\\n\\nSummary:
\\n- Current sprint length = 16\\n- ***State sync confirmation multiple = 8 (Proposed)***\\n- The block range to calculate the to value will increase from 16 to 128 (16*8)\\n\\n### Security Considerations\\n\\nThe proposed state sync confirmation multiple would lead to state sync transactions being delayed by ((128-16) * 2.25) ~ 252 seconds as the interval would be increased from 16 to 128 blocks.\\n\\n### Backward Compatibility\\n\\nThis PIP will not be backward compatible with the current implementation of Bor and will therefore require a ***Hard Fork***.\\n\\nIt should be considered that due to the use of sprint length within the `stateSyncConfirmationMultiplyer`, any future changes to the sprint length will require a revisit to the calculation logic.\\n\\n### Appendix\\n\\n* [PIP-7: Delhi Hardfork](https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-07.md)\\n\\n### Copyright \\nAll copyrights and related rights in this work are waived under [CC0 1.0 Universal](https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/legalcode).", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-10.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "Ahmedabad Hardfork", "summary": "Proposes the Ahmedabad Hardfork", "description": "### Abstract\\n\\nThis PIP specifies the changes included in the Polygon PoS hard fork named Ahmedabad.\\n\\n### Specification\\n\\n- Codename: Ahmedabad\\n\\n### Activation\\n\\n- Activation block to be added when consensus for the change achieved.\\n\\n### Included PIPs\\n\\n  *   [PIP-30: Increase Max Code Size Limit to 32KB](https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-30.md)\\n  *   [PIP-36: Replay Failed State Syncs](https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-36.md)\\n  *   [PIP-45: Token Symbol change](https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-45.md)\\n\\n### Copyright\\n\\nAll copyrights and related rights in this work are waived under CC0 1.0 Universal.", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-37.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "Polygon 2.0 Phase 0 - Frontier", "summary": "Specification of Polygon 2.0 - Phase 0", "description": "### Abstract\\n\\nThis proposal specifies Phase 0 of Polygon 2.0, a multi-phased upgrade to the Polygon Ecosystem. In Phase 0, the functioning of Polygon PoS and Polygon zkEVM are entirely unaffected for end users. Multiple changes are proposed to be implemented in the system smart contracts of these chains on Ethereum. In an homage to the original Ethereum release, we propose calling this initial phase of changes, “Frontier”. \\n\\nThe core of the proposed changes are:\\n\\n1.  The initiation of the POL upgrade\\n2.  The upgrade from MATIC to POL as the native (gas) token for PoS\\n3.  The adoption of POL as the staking token for Polygon PoS\\n4.  The launch of the Staking Layer and migration of Polygon public chains to consequently leverage it\\n    \\nWe propose a high-level outline for these changes, which should be deployed incrementally, with further detail on each coming through separate proposals to be published for community review.\\n\\n### Motivation\\n\\nPolygon 2.0 envisions a network of interconnected ZK-powered L2 chains that, on aggregate, expand Ethereum blockspace and create the Value Layer of the Internet. This environment is seamlessly interoperable, offering **access to unified blockspace** across all Polygon chains as well as infinite scalability. \\n\\n**The end goal is to extend Ethereum blockspace in a fashion that more resembles a mesh network topology like the internet**. With ZK technology, Ethereum blockspace can scale to the size of the Internet for the first time in blockchain history.\\n\\nFrom the start, the Polygon ecosystem has been committed to sustainably scaling Ethereum, bringing the idea of credibly neutral, decentralized blockchains to developers of all skill levels, while also ensuring that these blockchains remain cheap and accessible for all users. \\n\\nThis proposal represents the next step in this journey, defining the initial tasks to bring the Polygon 2.0 vision to life.\\n\\n### Specification\\n\\nPhase 0 is specifically designed to ensure that there will be no required changes for end users and developers already on the PoS or zkEVM chains. All proposed adjustments are targeted toward the various contracts and systems controlling the Polygon 1.0 ecosystem on Ethereum. \\n\\nThe following milestones will make up Phase 0:\\n\\n#### POL Upgrade\\n\\n-   Initiate upgrade of POL, a technical upgrade to MATIC, designed to power the Polygon 2.0 ecosystem.\\n-   Adopt POL as the native (gas) token for Polygon PoS. This will be accomplished by an upgrade to the Plasma Bridge, changing the MATIC withdrawal logic to only output POL.\\n-   Adopt POL as the staking token for Polygon PoS.\\n-   Upgrade the Polygon PoS validator rewards from MATIC to POL.\\n  \\n#### Staking Layer\\n\\n-   Launch the Staking Layer, a series of smart contracts that enable validators in the Polygon ecosystem to use their stake to participate in multiple services across the Polygon 2.0 ecosystem such as Validation, Sequencing, Proving, and Data Availability services for a variety of chains. \\n-   Migrate Polygon PoS staking and validation to a new service on the Staking Layer.\\n-   Migrate validator rewards from the legacy Polygon PoS staking contract to the new PoS Staking Layer service.\\n\\n![](https://lh4.googleusercontent.com/U1IpTIuLkykLmYkvot6IBNdO1NY6h3Kj_mQA1rh4dLfhKbmYI_Ml-aeeEk2kcFsJUpXrgRrs114OY6s_-6v7O15u71qin6g2juF_EFLPuZZtFj9dzo-bheH_k4zmq6kwSqhtBhLmzKBM-b5PlPVwEb4)\\n\\n### Backward Compatibility\\n\\nThis proposal seeks to lay out a roadmap that includes backwards incompatibility through the introduction, deprecation, and modification of Polygon system smart contracts on the Ethereum mainnet. As a result, this proposal covers a proposed order of operations and directly informs the creation of future PIPs to implement the functionality laid out in the “Specification” section. Relevant parties should carefully consider the changes laid out in this post, and monitor the eventual PIPs detailing them further to be prepared for any incompatibilities that will be introduced.\\n\\n### Security Considerations\\n\\nPolygon 2.0 is a significant technical upgrade of the Polygon system with broadscale changes to the PoS network, as well as the introduction of several novel systems. There are inherent risks in this migration, however, each component will undergo extensive testing, auditing, and public scrutiny prior to activation.  The staging of the PIPs and the methodical, piecemeal upgrade of the system is intended to ensure there is sufficient time for testing, auditing and scrutiny. \\n\\n### References\\n\\n-   [PIP-17: Polygon Ecosystem Token (POL)](https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-17.md)\\n-   [PIP-19: Update Polygon PoS Native Token to POL](https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-19.md)\\n\\n### Copyright\\n\\nAll copyrights and related rights in this work are waived under CC0 1.0 Universal.", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-18.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "Approve 4337 Wallet to join the DAO", "summary": "This test is to compliment the original proposal where we added the ERC-6551 NFT smart wallet as a member and let it submit a proposal.", "description": "

In this example, we are testing how the 4337 wallet interacts with DAOs. Sometimes, a DAO might not allow smart contracts and instead expects only an EOA(Externally Owned Account) like a regular Exodus or Metamask wallet. If the approval is successful, the EOA which controls the 4337 wallet will attempt to submit a proposal and see how it goes.

Based on the documentation attached, another test will be to see if transaction approvals are sent from the 4337 to the EOA or if it's all handled from the smart wallet itself. In the case of ERC-6551 NFTS with wallets, the requests of those accounts were sent to the EOA on the users phone or computer to be signed. We are learning together through actions and iteration. Future proposals may include a knowledge quest where a DAO is created only with 4337 wallets and an NFT will be issued for all participants.

Note: This DAO is on the Polygon blockchain and will require some MATIC to submit the proposal and be able to vote assuming adding the address goes as planned.

Tools:

Testing Device: iPhone 13 Mini
4337 Wallet: Ambre Wallet
Blockchain: Polygon

", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"ERC-4337 Docs\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-4337#motivation\\"}]"}

{"title": "Strategic Update on Reimbursement and Advancing Our Partnership with Traditional Dream Factory", "summary": "Dear Stakeholders,\\nAs we continue to forge a path towards sustainable development and innovative energy solutions, our collaboration with the Traditional Dream Factory (TDF) stands as a cornerstone of our strategy. The initial trip to TDF provided us with invaluable insights and set the stage for a transformative partnership. However, to fully realize the potential of this collaboration, we must address the crucial matter of our first reimbursement proposal.", "description": "

Importance of the First Reimbursement

The first reimbursement is not merely a procedural necessity; it is the key to unlocking further developmental milestones for WeRa Global and TDF. Unfortunately, the initial failure of this reimbursement proposal has stalled potential progress. Supporting this proposal is vital for several reasons:

  1. Enabling Further Engagement: The approval and support of the first reimbursement proposal are essential to fund a second business trip to TDF. This forthcoming visit is critical—it's where we intend to finalize our partnership agreement and concrete plans for the Solar Eco-Village.

  2. Demonstration of Commitment: By backing this reimbursement, our stakeholders demonstrate a commitment to our shared vision and to the practical steps required to implement it. This support is crucial for maintaining momentum and credibility, both within our community and in our partnership discussions with TDF.

  3. Leveraging Initial Investments: The funds from this reimbursement will directly enable us to capitalize on the groundwork laid during our initial visit. Every moment and resource invested thus far will culminate in this next, crucial phase: securing a partnership that promises significant mutual benefits.

Projected Benefits Post-Reimbursement

Supporting the reimbursement proposal ensures that WeRa Global can continue to harness the synergy between our innovative technologies and TDF’s sustainable practices. Here are the anticipated benefits of moving forward:

  • Enhanced Credibility and Trust: Finalizing our partnership with TDF will significantly boost our project's credibility, attracting further interest and investment. It demonstrates our ability to collaborate effectively with established players in the sustainability sector.

  • Content Generation and Community Engagement: The second trip will be a content-rich engagement, showcasing the real-time development of the Solar Network State and highlighting the practical implementation of our joint projects. This will not only inform but also inspire our community and potential investors about the tangible benefits of our initiatives.

  • Strategic Closure of Partnership Deal: The next visit is planned as a strategic move to close the partnership deal with TDF. This will solidify our collaborative efforts and ensure that the project moves from planning to execution, with all the necessary resources and commitments locked in.

Call to Action

We urge all stakeholders to recognize the importance of this first reimbursement. Your support will provide WeRa Global with the necessary resources to not only continue our pioneering work but also to secure and solidify a partnership that could redefine sustainable living and renewable energy landscapes. Let us ensure that the initial resources invested do not go to waste and that we can build on the solid foundation already laid.

Thank you for your attention and continued support. Together, we can achieve remarkable things.

Warm regards,

The WeRa Global Team

{"title": "Temporary Delegation of WCPe Tokens for Faster Upgrades", "summary": "We need to delegate WCPe tokens to the founder for one month. This will increase the founder's voting power in WeRa Capital DAO to allow our team to make decisions and execute technical upgrades immediately, without waiting for approval each time.", "description": "

Dear WeRa Global Members,

I hope you are all doing well. I am writing to ask for your support in making our development process faster and more efficient.
Why This is Important: Right now, every technical upgrade and test we do takes 3 days because of our current approval process. This slows us down a lot.

What We Need: We need to delegate WCPe tokens to the founder signature for one month. This will increase the founder's voting power in WeRa Capital DAO to allow our team to make decisions and execute technical upgrades immediately, without waiting for approval each time.

Benefits:

  • Speed: Immediate upgrades and tests.

  • Efficiency: Faster development means we can improve the platform quicker.

  • Better Service: Quicker response to any technical issues.

Important Details:

  • The WeRa Global DAO will still hold 78% of WCPe tokens.

  • The Global DAO can always return the delegated tokens if needed without approval from the founder.

  • This action will showcase the governance capacity of our platform to delegate ownership rights for execution of specific projects.

Conclusion:
This small change will help us move faster and serve our community better.
Please vote to approve this proposal.

Thank you for your support!

Very truly yours,
Mike AF

{"title": "Continue the SOLVE 2.0 Farming Campaign", "summary": "Continue the farming campaign via Merkl for 28 more days.", "description": "

This proposal, should it pass, will continue the farming campaign for an additional 28 days via Merkl.

SOLVE 2.0 tokenomics included a distribution of 2M SOLVE tokens through farming. The initial farming campaign was only for 7 days to test the system and ensure users can participate easily and efficiently. The first week was successful so this proposal will continue the campaign for another 28 days.

By extending the campaign from 7 days to 28 days it can attract more current holders and potentially attract non-token holders to participate as the campaign is longer term. Putting 28 days worth of rewards into Merkl does have a higher risk than putting 7 days worth of tokens in but after further research it is clear that security is a top priority of Merkl. This proposal does not warrant putting all 2M tokens into the Merkl contract at once but will add an additional 156K SOLVE tokens to continue the farming campaign for 28 days.

Should this proposal pass the farming campaign will begin the same day. The voting has been set so once the unanimous decision is made, the proposal will immediately pass. This will reduce the gap between the first campaign and the continuation.

Tokens will be added to the Merkl contract via the steering committee.

{"title": "Proposal for Submission Channels for Enhanced Community Engagement", "summary": "This proposal recommends the establishment of multiple new channels for the SOLVE community to submit and discuss proposals. By leveraging platforms such as the Solve.Care subreddit, Discord, Telegram, nd additional platforms yet to be chosen, we aim to facilitate more accessible, diverse, and dynamic engagement within our community, enhancing the decentralization and inclusivity of the SOLVE 2.0 governance model.", "description": "

To support the growing involvement and innovation within the SOLVE community, it is essential to provide varied and user-friendly platforms for proposal submissions and discussions. This proposal outlines the creation of dedicated spaces across several popular platforms, along with the introduction of new platforms in the future, allowing community members to easily contribute, collaborate, and comment on governance and development initiatives.

Proposed Channels and Implementation:

Monthly Thread on Solve.Care Subreddit:

  • Purpose: To host a recurring, structured discussion thread where community members can post, review, and comment on proposals.

  • Implementation: A new thread will be created at the beginning of each month by subreddit moderators, where members can submit proposals as comments.

Dedicated Proposal Channel on Discord:

  • Purpose: To facilitate real-time discussion and brainstorming sessions regarding new proposals.

  • Implementation: Creation of a new channel within the existing SOLVE Discord server specifically for discussing ongoing proposals and ideas before formal submission.

Proposal Discussion Subgroup on Telegram:

  • Purpose: To provide a more immediate and accessible platform for proposal discussions, allowing for broader participation and quicker feedback loops.

  • Implementation: Set up a subgroup linked to the main SOLVE Telegram group, focusing exclusively on proposal discussions and updates.

Other Platforms:

  • Purpose: To ensure all community members have access to proposal submission and discussion channels that best suit their needs and preferences.

  • Implementation: The selection of additional platforms will be based on community feedback and technological suitability to ensure effective engagement across all demographics of the SOLVE community.

Expected Outcomes:

Implementing these channels will:

  1. Enhance the transparency and traceability of proposal submissions and discussions.

  2. Encourage continuous community participation by providing various platforms suited to different preferences and technical proficiencies.

  3. Streamline the proposal review and approval processes, making them more efficient and inclusive.

Conclusion

The proposed expansion of proposal submission channels reflects our commitment to fostering an open, inclusive, and participative community governance structure. By introducing these new channels, and planning for more in the future, we aim to lower the barriers to participation, ensuring that every community member has the opportunity to contribute to the governance and evolution of SOLVE 2.0.

We seek the support of the SOLVE community to implement these channels, thereby enriching our community engagement and enhancing the democratic process within our ecosystem.

This proposal outlines the creation of dedicated spaces across several popular platforms, along with the introduction of new platforms in the future, allowing community members to easily contribute, collaborate, and comment on governance and development initiatives.

Proposed Channels and Implementation:

Monthly Thread on Solve.Care Subreddit:

  • Purpose: To host a recurring, structured discussion thread where community members can post, review, and comment on proposals.

  • Implementation: A new thread will be created at the beginning of each month by subreddit moderators, where members can submit proposals as comments.

Dedicated Proposal Channel on Discord:

  • Purpose: To facilitate real-time discussion and brainstorming sessions regarding new proposals.

  • Implementation: Creation of a new channel within the existing SOLVE Discord server specifically for discussing ongoing proposals and ideas before formal submission.

Proposal Discussion Subgroup on Telegram:

  • Purpose: To provide a more immediate and accessible platform for proposal discussions, allowing for broader participation and quicker feedback loops.

  • Implementation: Set up a subgroup linked to the main SOLVE Telegram group, focusing exclusively on proposal discussions and updates.

Other Platforms:

  • Purpose: To ensure all community members have access to proposal submission and discussion channels that best suit their needs and preferences.

  • Implementation: The selection of additional platforms will be based on community feedback and technological suitability to ensure effective engagement across all demographics of the SOLVE community.

Expected Outcomes:

Implementing these channels will:

  1. Enhance the transparency and traceability of proposal submissions and discussions.

  2. Encourage continuous community participation by providing various platforms suited to different preferences and technical proficiencies.

  3. Streamline the proposal review and approval processes, making them more efficient and inclusive.

Conclusion

The proposed expansion of proposal submission channels reflects our commitment to fostering an open, inclusive, and participative community governance structure. By introducing these new channels, and planning for more in the future, we aim to lower the barriers to participation, ensuring that every community member has the opportunity to contribute to the governance and evolution of SOLVE 2.0.

We seek the support of the SOLVE community to implement these channels, thereby enriching our community engagement and enhancing the democratic process within our ecosystem.

{"title": "Proposal for Expansion of the SOLVE 2.0 Steering Committee", "summary": "This proposal seeks the inclusion of two additional members from the SOLVE community to the existing SOLVE 2.0 Steering Committee. This initiative aims to widen the representation on the committee from three to five members, thereby enhancing decision-making diversity and community engagement in line with the transformative ethos of SOLVE 2.0.", "description": "

In the spirit of decentralized governance and community involvement that SOLVE 2.0 embodies, it is proposed that the Steering Committee be expanded to include two more members elected from the SOLVE community. This change will increase the number of committee members from three to five, providing a broader spectrum of insights and strengthening the community's voice in governance processes.

Current Structure and Rationale for Change:

The current three-member format of the Steering Committee is good for initiating the foundational governance framework of SOLVE 2.0. However, as the platform continues to evolve and expand, a more inclusive governance structure is necessary to mirror the diverse perspectives and needs of our community. This is in line with our commitment to foster an environment that values community input and decentralized decision-making.


Selection Process:

The two new members will be elected through an open and transparent voting process involving the SOLVE community. Eligibility for candidacy will be determined based on active participation in the community and a demonstrated understanding of blockchain technologies and healthcare applications. The election process will be supervised by a neutral third party to ensure fairness and transparency. The deadline for the election and inclusion of the new members is set for May 2nd, 2024.


Roles and Responsibilities:

The expanded committee will continue to oversee the strategic direction of SOLVE 2.0, including but not limited to:

  • Reviewing and approving changes.

  • Making decisions on community fund allocations.

  • Guiding the development and implementation of new network features.

  • Ensuring the alignment of initiatives with the overall mission of Solve.Care.


Expected Outcomes:

By involving community-elected members, we expect to achieve higher levels of engagement and satisfaction within the SOLVE community, ensuring that our governance model reflects the needs and expectations of its members. This inclusivity is anticipated to enhance the robustness of decision-making processes and foster a greater sense of ownership and commitment across the community.


Conclusion:

The expansion of the SOLVE 2.0 Steering Committee is a natural step in our evolution towards a more decentralized and community-driven governance model. We believe this change will not only enhance the effectiveness of the committee but also reinforce our commitment to a democratic governance structure that values the contributions of all stakeholders.

We seek the support of the SOLVE community to bring this proposal to fruition, aligning our governance practices with our vision for a decentralized future in healthcare innovation. Your approval of this proposal by April 24th, 2024 will be instrumental in shaping a participative and responsive governance framework for SOLVE 2.0.
