{"title": "Phased expansion of Validator Slots", "summary": "Proposes increasing validator slots to 125", "description": "### Abstract\\n\\nExpanding the number of validators within the Polygon PoS network is a crucial step toward enhancing decentralization. By increasing the number of validators, validation responsibilities are distributed more widely, which reduces the risk of centralization and strengthens the network's security and resilience. This proposal outlines the considerations for expanding the number of validator slots to ensure robust network performance.\\n\\n### Motivation\\n\\nThe primary motivation for increasing the number of validator slots within the Polygon PoS network is to improve decentralization which can be observed via the following metrics:\\n\\n- **Geographical Distribution of Nodes**: Expanding the number of validator slots allows for a wider geographical distribution of nodes. By having validators in diverse locations globally, the risk of localized disruptions is reduced, and the network's health is enhanced to prevent regional failures.\\n- **Decrease Stake Centralisation**: More validator slots means that voting power and block production can be more evenly distributed across a larger number of validators.\\n- **Improve Nakamoto Coefficient**: The Nakamoto Coefficient, which measures the minimum number of validators required to compromise a network, should improve with an increase in validator nodes. A higher coefficient indicates a more secure and decentralized network, as it becomes harder for any group to disrupt the network's consensus.\\n\\n### Specification\\n\\nThe proposed expansion involves a phased increase in the number of validator slots, starting from the current 105 slots to 110, then to 115, further to 120 and finally to 125. This incremental approach, with ongoing community review and feedback, allows for careful monitoring and management of each expansion stage. The specific steps include:\\n\\n1. Assessing the current infrastructure and ensuring it can support the additional validators.\\n2. Implementing the first phase by adding 5 validator slots and monitoring the network's performance and stability.\\n3. Gradually increasing the slots in subsequent phases (as noted above) based on performance metrics and network impact.\\n4. Conducting thorough monitoring at each stage to ensure seamless integration and functionality.\\n5. In the event that the expansion from 105 to 110 validators negatively impacts the network's health, the community may consider taking corrective action to offboard the last 5 validators who were onboarded.\\n\\n### Rationale\\n\\nIncreasing the number of validator slots in phases offers several advantages:\\n\\n- It helps the network grow gradually, lowering the risks that come with adding many new validators all at once.\\n- Each phase can be evaluated for performance, ensuring that any issues are identified and addressed promptly.\\n- It ensures that the network infrastructure and support mechanisms are adequately scaled to handle the increased load, thereby maintaining overall network health and performance.\\n\\n### Backwards Compatibility\\n\\nThe expansion of validator slots is designed to be fully backwards compatible. Existing validators and network operations should not be affected by the increase in slots. The system architecture should ensure that the integration of new validators occurs smoothly, without disrupting ongoing operations or diminishing the performance of current validators.\\n\\n### Test Cases\\n\\nTo ensure the success of the validator slot expansion, the following test cases will be conducted:\\n\\n- _Performance Testing_: Evaluate the network's ability to handle the increased number of validators, focusing on transaction processing times, block generation, and overall output.\\n- _Stability Testing_: Monitor the network for any signs of instability or degradation in performance following the addition of new validators.\\n\\n### Security Considerations\\n\\nSecurity remains a top priority during the expansion process. Security measures will include:\\n\\n- Enhanced monitoring and alerting systems to detect and respond to any abnormal activity.\\n- Suggested implementation of best practices for validator operations.\\n\\n### Copyright\\n\\nAll copyrights and related rights in this work are waived under CC0 1.0 Universal.", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-38.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "Validator Admissions into PoS Network", "summary": "Proposes Validator Admission Framework", "description": "### Abstract\\n\\n[PIP-4](https://forum.polygon.technology/t/pip-4-validator-performance-management/9956) Part C, outlines the path of Validator Admissions into the network. This proposal outlines an admissions framework that includes a set of objective parameters to determine a prospective validator's eligibility to validate the PoS network.\\n\\n### Motivation\\n\\nA healthy network requires healthy validators/operators. The aim of a validator admission process is to:\\n1) ensure a competent set of validators on PoS with greater transparency and community input on validator admissions, and\\n2) ensure network security, reliability and uptime.\\n\\nHaving a quantitative, objective process in place will ensure that those who are becoming part of the network are experienced and able to participate at a high rate of adherence to on-chain operations.\\n\\nA large, mature and widely used network like the PoS chain, requires quality validators who uphold reliability and performance to meet the demands of the industry in which they operate.\\n\\nThe proposed admissions process assists in providing the network with a framework to evaluate potential validators who request to join the PoS network. This process scores and ranks applicants based on the evaluation criteria described in the specification.\\n\\n### Rationale\\n\\nIn accordance with PIP-4, a scoring system that will rank validator applicants is proposed, allowing only the highest-scoring candidates to be selected for onboarding to the network.\\n\\nTo define the scoring system, identified below are characteristics of validators that correlate with high performance; utilizing a number of heuristics based on the correlation matrix.\\n\\nThe scoring is composed of the three following factors:\\n\\n1. **Stake**\\n\\nMATIC stake to provide enhanced network security.\\n\\n2. **Experience**\\n\\nNumber of years operating a validating node on a PoS network.\\n\\n3. **Expertise**\\n\\nValidator operators score on an assessment test.\\n\\nThe correlation matrix below shows a moderately positive correlation between PoS validator experience and performance. In other words, validators with more experience tend to perform better.\\n\\nBased on these findings, adjustments were made to the scoring logic used to evaluate the prospective validators. These adjustments ensured a fair and accurate performance assessment, considering their experience and expertise.\\n\\nIt is important to note that while experience was found to have a high correlation with performance, stake and expertise also played significant roles in determining a validator's effectiveness. Therefore, a holistic approach was taken to consider multiple factors in the scoring logic adjustments.\\n\\nUsing the insights gained from this survey, the evaluation process was improved, and informed decisions regarding the validators' contributions and rewards can continue to be made.\\n\\n![|624x221](https://lh7-eu.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXe40GRphZCkLCGLph7wiKkoaabo2gIT-w-xaxo-5kXBSJ9HADRjvp9QrSu6jTkBpftc0l3VDaSbotwAvzAYVd681RXHxzmP-3G1AcN1TjJ0UCN-egkcwbRyS5mM-4HXIBshjRnlOhzwE1I4UKg_IbVASctj?key=WpqSAzkRRGAd_g58htzluQ)\\n\\nThe Pearson correlation coefficient was used to quantify the relationship between performance and experience for Polygon PoS’s current validators, which measures the linear association between two variables. It has a value between -1 and 1 where:\\n\\n-1 indicates a perfectly negative linear correlation between two variables\\n\\n0 indicates no linear correlation between the two variables\\n\\n1 indicates a perfectly positive linear correlation between two variables\\n\\nThe further away the correlation coefficient is from zero, the stronger the relationship between the two variables.\\n\\n#### Results:\\n\\nThus far, 12 entities have been recently onboarded to the network through the new admissions process, with zero breakages or bugs in the test phase.\\n\\nTo illustrate the effectiveness of this process, below is data referring to the amount of times these validators fell into Grace Period 1 in their first 2100 checkpoints after joining the network.\\n\\n**The [PIP4 performance management process](https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-04.md) outlines that if a validator falls below a 95% performance in signing checkpoints, they move into Grace Period 1. This Grace Period is a 700 checkpoint window in which they have to regain 95% adherence. If they once again fail to meet the 95% adherence requirement, they fall into Grace Period 2, another 700 checkpoint period. If they then again fail to meet this 95% requirement they then receive Final Notice and are removed from the network.*\\n\\nRegaining adherence moves them straight back into healthy status.\\n\\nThe following table shows the validators who were recently onboarded through this Admissions Process and the corresponding times they fell into Grace Period 1 in their first 2100 checkpoints in the network. Only 3 of these 12 validators have entered the grace period, reflecting a healthy admission process.\\n\\n|Validator ID|Name|# of times in GP1|Currently in active set|\\n| --- | --- | --- | --- |\\n|170|Sensei Node|1|Yes|\\n|156|Nexon|0|No|\\n|163|Upbit|0|Yes|\\n|161|Northstake|2|Yes|\\n|152|Zee Prime|1|No|\\n|162|P2P Network|0|Yes|\\n|164|Validatrium|0|Yes|\\n|165|Simply Staking|0|Yes|\\n|159|Mantra DAO|0|Yes|\\n|160|NEOPIN|0|Yes|\\n|167|Ryabina|0|Yes|\\n\\nNotes:\\n\\n[1. Update: PoS Validator Admissions](https://forum.polygon.technology/t/update-pos-validator-admissions/12344)\\n\\n[2. PoS Admissions Update](https://forum.polygon.technology/t/pos-admissions-update/11348)\\n\\n#### Impact:\\n\\nAll of these validators have performed very well since joining. Those that have left the active set have left for reasons other than performance. A direct correlation has been shown between these validators' scores and their ability to perform validation services in the network.\\n\\n### Specification\\n\\n#### Scoring Logic\\n\\nThe final scoring logic is outlined below:\\n\\n1. Stake: 45%\\n2. Experience: 25%\\n3. Expertise: 30%\\n\\nThe expertise section is categorized into 3 parts:\\n\\n* Part 1: Technical Assessment - 9 Questions +1 for every correct response. No negative scoring.\\n* Part 2: Governance Assessment - 5 Questions +1 for every correct response. No negative scoring.\\n* Part 3: Outsourced Operations +1 if none of the validator operations are outsourced. No negative scoring.\\n\\nTo avoid foul play, the following measures have been introduced:\\n\\n1. All assessment questions will be randomized and changed periodically\\n2. All assessment questions will be timed\\n3. All assessment answer options will be randomised\\n\\nHighly scored validators across these 3 areas can be considered eligible. An eligible score does not guarantee admission. Admission is subject to verification.\\n\\n### Backwards compatibility\\n\\nThis proposal does not introduce any breaking changes and is therefore backwards compatible.\\n\\n### Security Considerations\\n\\nThe admissions process takes into account an applicant's hosting region, on-chain activity in other protocols and general security practices around their hardware (i.e., how they operate their own internal systems).\\n\\nThe implementation of this PIP proves no direct security risk to the protocol.\\n\\n### Copyright\\n\\nAll copyrights and related rights in this work are waived under [CCO 1.0 Universal](https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/legalcode).\\n\\n### Appendix\\n\\nThe scoring logic and weights were an extensive exercise in decision-making, impactfully combining data and observation.\\n\\n**Activity 1:** A survey of high-performing validators measured the correlation between stake, experience, expertise, and performance.\\n**Activity 2:** A community study to mathematically measure and define the three parameters of evaluation: Experience, Expertise and Stake.\\n\\nExperience: > 3 years as a PoS validator considered as high experience\\nExpertise: High percentile in the validator assessment quiz (designed to test Polygon PoS understanding)\\n\\nStake: Meeting the minimum staking requirement of the network to ensure economic security", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-39.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "Upgrade cosmos-sdk in heimdall", "summary": "Proposes an upgrade for heimdall by bumping cosmos-sdk dependency", "description": "### Abstract\\n\\nCurrently, Heimdall uses a fork of `cosmos-sdk`, which has been modified to support the specific needs of Polygon PoS, and is based on version `v0.37.4`. We propose a refactoring that will upgrade such dependency to target version `v0.50.x`.\\n\\n### Motivation\\n\\nThe primary motivation for such an upgrade is to remove tech debt currently anchoring Heimdall to an outdated `cosmos-sdk` version released around four years ago. `v0.37.4` is also no longer supported by the team that stewards the Cosmos SDK (EOL version).\\n\\n### Specification\\n\\nThis proposal integrates ABCI v2.0 (a.k.a. ABCI++) into Heimdall, enhancing the interaction between the consensus layer (CometBFT) and the application layer. ABCI v2.0 introduces a more granular interface that allows the application to interact with the consensus process during block proposal, voting, and finalization phases, providing significant improvements in flexibility, performance, and security.  \\n\\n**Overview of ABCI v2.0**  \\n\\nABCI (Application Blockchain Interface) is the protocol layer between CometBFT and the application. In the current system (ABCI v0.17), the application passively processes finalized blocks through three main calls:  \\n1. **BeginBlock**: Initializes block processing.  \\n2. **DeliverTx**: Executes transactions.  \\n3. **EndBlock**: Finalizes the block state.  \\n\\nABCI v2.0 extends this model by enabling the application to actively participate in multiple phases of the block lifecycle:  \\n\\n1. **Block Proposal Phase**:\\nDuring this phase, the application can inspect and validate proposed blocks, as well as modify the transactions to be proposed, before blocks are finalized.  \\n\\n2. **Voting Phase**:\\nValidators vote in two sub-phases:\\n   - **Prevote**: Validators signal preliminary agreement on the block.\\n   - **Precommit**: Validators confirm their agreement.\\n\\n3. The application can provide custom validation logic during these phases.\\n4. **Finalization Phase**:  \\nAfter consensus is reached, the finalized block is committed to the chain, and the application finalizes state transitions.\\n\\n![ABCI++ functions](./../assets/PIP-44/comet.png)\\n\\n### Backwards Compatibility\\n\\nThe upgrade is incompatible with the current Heimdall chain. This means that launching a newer version of cosmos-sdk within Polygon PoS will only be possible by migrating Heimdall to a brand new chain. We will be able to export the current state of Heimdall into the genesis for the new Heimdall chain. History at the CometBFT level (transactions, blocks, validator sets, etc) cannot persist via the upgrade workflow. To mitigate this, archive nodes can be made available so that the community can access CometBFT (actually, Peppermint) history before the migration point. Those archive nodes are simply non-validating full nodes running the current version of Heimdall.\\n\\n### Security considerations\\n\\nThe upgrade involves a complete refactoring of the Heimdall codebase. Hence, Heimdall will undergo a meticulous phase of tests, audits, and performance reviews. Despite the many new functionalities offered by the new library, this migration will be—at first—a 1:1 migration, keeping intact the Heimdall capabilities and utilities.\\n\\n\\n### Copyright\\n\\nAll copyrights and related rights in this work are waived under CC0 1.0 Universal.", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-44.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "Protocol Council Multi-Threshold Smart Contract Upgrades", "summary": "Proposes a multi-threshold upgradability mechanism for the Protocol Council", "description": "## Abstract\\n\\nThis proposal introduces a multi-threshold multisig system to the Protocol Council governing body that builds upon Aragon's OSx protocol, enhancing access control while ensuring extensibility, in line with [PIP-29: Polygon Protocol Council](https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-29.md) specification.\\n\\nThe proposed contracts integrate the flexibility of multiple approval thresholds, improving upon traditional dual multisig setups by allowing fine-grained permissions and proposal execution control.\\n\\n## Motivation\\n\\nCurrent dual-contract multisig setups often introduce inefficiencies in access control by requiring additional roles and permissions for contract-based governance. This can lead to complexity in managing contracts and reduce flexibility in decision-making processes.\\n\\nThe proposed multi-threshold multisig offers a streamlined solution for the Protocol Council by eliminating the need for new roles and instead leveraging the existing permission structures within the system. This significantly reduces friction in access control management, making the system more efficient and easier to use.\\n\\nMulti-threshold upgradeability also follows the specification laid out for the Protocol Council in [PIP-29](https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-29.md):\\n\\n>Regular, i.e., non-emergency, changes to the contracts are facilitated by a 7/13 (55%) consensus to maximize efficiency. These types of changes require a timelock delay of 10 days to ensure the ability for the community to exit the system before any change takes place.\\n\\n>At the same time, an emergency route can facilitate immediate changes to system smart contracts in case of a critical issue. A timelock mechanism proves inefficient in such scenarios, as it’s unable to ensure any potential issue remains contained and is addressed immediately without opportunities for outside interference, due to the public nature of timelocked transactions. Consequently, an emergency route for changing system smart contracts is introduced, requiring a very high 10/13 consensus of the Protocol Council to execute any change without a timelock.\\n\\n## Definitions\\n\\n**Multi-Threshold Multisig**: A governance system that requires varying approval thresholds depending on the context of the proposal.\\n\\n**Timelock**: A delay mechanism that ensures a waiting period before executing certain actions or proposals. This is primarily used for non-emergency proposals to provide time for community review.\\n\\n**Aragon OSx**: A modular contract suite for decentralized decision governance and permission management written in Solidity. It allows for composable governance structures using plugins to easily upgrade functionalities without disrupting operations.\\n\\n**Executor (DAO.sol)**: The contract responsible for holding permissions and executing actions. It verifies whether an address has the required permissions to perform a certain operation.\\n\\n**Governor**: The multisig component of the system is responsible for creating proposals, gathering approvals, and executing actions once the required number of approvals is met. It works alongside the Executor to ensure the correct functioning of the multisig system.\\n\\n**Plugin Setup Contract**: A contract that handles the installation and configuration of the multisig plugin within the executor contract (DAO.sol). It assigns permissions and ensures that all components of the multisig system are correctly connected.\\n\\n**PermissionManager**: A part of the system that manages permissions for different roles and actions. It controls who can perform certain actions within the system, ensuring secure and decentralized access control.\\n\\n**allowFailureMap**: A feature that allows certain actions within a proposal to fail without reverting the entire transaction. This provides more flexibility in the execution of complex proposals by permitting non-critical failures.\\n\\n**Emergency Proposal**: A type of proposal that requires immediate execution due to a critical situation. It is subject to higher approval thresholds and may bypass the usual timelock or approval process to address urgent issues quickly.\\n\\n## Specification\\n\\nThe multi-threshold multisig is built on top of Aragon’s OSx protocol, following the typical Solidity topology of Executor and Governor contracts with a Plugin Setup contract to ensure correct functioning of the plugin in the different stages of its lifecycle.\\n\\n![|452x281](https://lh7-qw.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXenjlFcNUEh3Wr0WlFDNjIOicJVLjLXn6NG7y0e6CH0DWQqPEE-HUEYFm4fot6mX8n0fGQM1Bp7rpw4uOozblBmFEaF7O2I6UbhgM8KHD-4y859Ivy8tR9e-ILVeo-KOzqokHOl6hDWIFkmTvrOPiSIqFw?key=b8h055Jyd32Zp6xNQ9BT6g)\\n\\nThe `DAO.sol` contract serves two main purposes:\\n\\n* Hold the list of the permissions assigned\\n* Execute on behalf of the contracts that own that permission\\n\\nThe function `hasPermission()` checks if an address has permission on a contract via a permission identifier and considers if `ANY_ADDRESS` was used in the granting process.\\n\\n```solidity\\nfunction hasPermission(\\n\\n    address _where,\\n\\n    address _who,\\n\\n    bytes32 _permissionId,\\n\\n    bytes memory _data\\n\\n) external view returns (bool);\\n```\\n\\nThe input parameters descriptions are the following:\\n\\n* `_where`: The address of the contract.\\n* `_who`: The address of an EOA or contract to give the permissions.\\n* `_permissionId`: The permission identifier.\\n* `_data`: The optional data passed to the `PermissionCondition` registered.\\n\\nThe second most important functionality in this contract is the `execute()` function doing so for a list of Actions. If a zero allow-failure map is provided, a failing action reverts the entire execution. If a non-zero allow-failure map is provided, allowed actions can fail without the entire call being reverted.\\n\\n``` solidity\\nfunction execute(\\n\\n    bytes32 _callId,\\n\\n    Action[] memory _actions,\\n\\n    uint256 _allowFailureMap\\n\\n) external returns (bytes[] memory, uint256);\\n```\\n\\nThe input parameters descriptions are the following:\\n\\n* `_callId`: The ID of the call. The definition of the value of `callId` is up to the calling contract and can be used, e.g., as a nonce.\\n* `_actions`: The array of actions.\\n* `_allowFailureMap`: A bitmap allowing execution to succeed, even if individual actions might revert. If the bit at index `i` is `1`, the execution succeeds even if `i` action reverts. A failure map value of `0` requires every action not to revert.\\n\\nAs stated before, the `DAO.sol` (also referred to as the Executor) is connected to the Multi-threshold Multisig (Governor), and it does so by the permissions stored in the first contract. These permissions are set at “installation” time by the `PluginSetup`, which tells the `DAO.sol` which permissions should be granted from whom and to who for the correct functioning of the system. It does so by deploying a specific instance of the Multisig contract for the `DAO.sol` and then using the `DAO.sol` Permission Manager to assign the required Operations.\\n\\n``` solidity\\n// Prepare permissions\\n\\nPermissionLib.MultiTargetPermission[]\\n\\nmemory permissions = new PermissionLib.MultiTargetPermission[](3);\\n\\n// Set permissions to be granted.\\n\\n// Grant the list of permissions of the plugin to the DAO.\\n\\npermissions[0] = PermissionLib.MultiTargetPermission(\\n\\nPermissionLib.Operation.Grant,\\n\\nplugin,\\n\\n_dao,\\n\\nPermissionLib.NO_CONDITION,\\n\\nmultisigBase.UPDATE_MULTISIG_SETTINGS_PERMISSION_ID()\\n\\n);\\n\\npermissions[1] = PermissionLib.MultiTargetPermission(\\n\\nPermissionLib.Operation.Grant,\\n\\nplugin,\\n\\n_dao,\\n\\nPermissionLib.NO_CONDITION,\\n\\nmultisigBase.UPGRADE_PLUGIN_PERMISSION_ID()\\n\\n);\\n\\n// Grant `EXECUTE_PERMISSION` of the DAO to the plugin.\\n\\npermissions[2] = PermissionLib.MultiTargetPermission(\\n\\nPermissionLib.Operation.Grant,\\n\\n_dao,\\n\\nplugin,\\n\\nPermissionLib.NO_CONDITION,\\n\\nDAO(payable(_dao)).EXECUTE_PERMISSION_ID()\\n\\n);\\n```\\n\\nWith that, we have the Executor connected to the Governor, that is, the `DAO.sol` contract with the Multisig Plugin.\\n\\nNow, since `DAO.sol` doesn’t hold any logic by itself to tell it when to execute something, that’s delegated to the Multisig, which has the functionality to create proposals, approve them, wait, and confirm their execution.\\n\\n``` solidity\\nfunction createProposal(\\n\\n    bytes calldata _metadata,\\n\\n    IDAO.Action[] calldata _actions,\\n\\n    uint256 _allowFailureMap,\\n\\n    bool _approveProposal,\\n\\n    uint64 _startDate,\\n\\n    uint64 _endDate,\\n\\n    bool _emergency\\n\\n) external returns (uint256 proposalId);\\n```\\n\\nWhen creating a proposal, the multisig members are required to pass certain parameters in. The first, the metadata is an IPFS cid, in which members can pass any important offchain data that helps users understand the proposal being discussed. The file stored in IPFS should be in json format, and while it’s open for anyone to input whatever is desired, it is recommended having at least the following keys:\\n\\n``` solidity\\nmetadata: {\\n\\ntitle: string;\\n\\nsummary: string;\\n\\ntype: string;\\n\\ndescription?: string;\\n\\nresources: Array<{\\n\\nname: string;\\n\\nurl: string;\\n\\n}>;\\n\\n};\\n```\\n\\nFor the rest of the parameters of the proposal creation function:\\n\\n* `_actions`: The actions that will be executed after the proposal passes.\\n* `_allowFailureMap`: A bitmap allowing the proposal to succeed, even if individual actions might revert. If the bit at index `i` is `1`, the proposal succeeds even if the `i` action reverts. A failure map value of `0` requires every action to not revert.\\n* `_approveProposal`: If `true`, the sender will approve the proposal.\\n* `_startDate`: The start date of the proposal.\\n* `_endDate`: The end date of the proposal.\\n* `_emergency`: Whether the proposal is an emergency proposal or not.\\n\\nAt this point, when the data is being stored, the plugin parameters get saved into the proposal itself, freezing any changes from misconfiguring the proposal. These plugin-level parameters are the following:\\n\\n``` solidity \\nstruct MultisigSettings {\\n\\nbool onlyListed = True;\\n\\nuint16 minApprovals = 7;\\n\\nuint16 emergencyMinApprovals = 10;\\n\\nuint64 delayDuration = 10 days;\\n\\nbool memberOnlyProposalExecution = True;\\n\\nuint256 minExtraDuration = 7 days;\\n\\n}\\n```\\n\\nThe plugin parameters are set by governance, and can be changed at the plugin level by a proposal passed by the council.\\n\\nThe parameters function as follows:\\n\\n* `onlyListed`: Whether only listed members can create proposals.\\n* `minApprovals`: The minimal number of approvals required for a proposal to pass\\n* `emergencyMinApprovals`: The minimal number of approvals required for an emergency proposal to pass.\\n* `delayDuration`: The duration of the timelock between proposals has been approved and then confirmed at a later date.\\n* `memberOnlyProposalExecution`: Boolean to set if only multi-sig members should be allowed to execute once the proposal is finished and confirmed\\n* `minExtraDuration`: The minimal extra duration for members to approve and confirm the proposal. This prevents proposals from being created with a timespan that won’t allow the council to participate effectively.\\n\\nOnce a proposal has been created, the first stage starts, and it’s the same whether the proposal is an emergency one or not: enough approvals need to be gathered in time for the proposal to proceed.\\n\\n``` solidity\\naddress approver = _msgSender();\\n\\nif (!_canApprove(_proposalId, approver)) {\\n\\nrevert ApprovalCastForbidden(_proposalId, approver);\\n\\n}\\n\\nProposal storage proposal_ = proposals[_proposalId];\\n\\nunchecked {\\n\\nproposal_.approvals += 1;\\n\\n}\\n\\nproposal_.approvers[approver] = true;\\n```\\n\\nOnce the required number of approvals is met, there are two possible execution routes:\\n\\n### Emergency\\n\\nCan be executed by calling the `execute()` function:\\n\\n```solidity\\nreturn\\n\\nproposal_.parameters.emergency\\n\\n? proposal_.approvals >= proposal_.parameters.emergencyMinApprovals\\n\\n: (proposal_.approvals >= proposal_.parameters.minApprovals &&\\n\\nproposal_.confirmations >= proposal_.parameters.minApprovals);\\n```\\n\\nThe proposal is not an emergency, and therefore, it requires following the normal flow in which members upload the secondary metadata and start the timelock specified by the plugin settings.\\n\\nAfter the timelock has expired, a second confirmation is required from the multisig members.\\n\\n```solidity\\naddress approver = _msgSender();\\n\\nif (!_canConfirm(_proposalId, approver)) {\\n\\nrevert ConfirmationCastForbidden(_proposalId, approver);\\n\\n}\\n\\nProposal storage proposal_ = proposals[_proposalId];\\n\\nunchecked {\\n\\nproposal_.confirmations += 1;\\n\\n}\\n\\nproposal_.confirmation_approvers[approver] = true;\\n```\\n\\nAfter that, the process reaches its final point, in which can be executed if the required number of members have approved and confirmed the proposal in time:\\n\\n```solidity\\nProposal storage proposal_ = proposals[_proposalId];\\nproposal_.executed = true;\\n\\n_executeProposal(\\n\\ndao(),\\n\\n_proposalId,\\n\\nproposals[_proposalId].actions,\\n\\nproposals[_proposalId].allowFailureMap\\n\\n);\\n```\\n\\nThis execution ties back to the initial step, where the `DAO.sol` contract receives a set of actions to execute, along with a failure map. The failure map allows for greater granularity in handling the atomicity of these executions.\\n\\n## Backward Compatibility\\n\\nThe proposed multi-threshold multisig system introduces new functionalities that require upgrades to existing contracts.\\n\\nSpecifically, the new multisig system requires updates to the roles and permissions managed by existing [Protocol Council](https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-29.md) multisig contracts.\\n\\n## Security Considerations\\n\\nThe primary security challenge is ensuring that the system can handle varying approval thresholds and partial action failures without introducing vulnerabilities that could be exploited.\\n\\nAdditionally, the proposal system's staged approval mechanism, requiring both an initial and a final confirmation for non-emergency proposals, adds an additional layer of security by preventing premature execution of unauthorized actions.\\n\\nTo mitigate edge cases, the system includes expiry mechanisms for approvals and confirmations, ensuring that proposals cannot linger indefinitely in an approved state and become vulnerable to future exploits.\\n\\nFurther real-world testing will occur during phased deployments to ensure all potential issues are identified and mitigated.\\n\\n## Copyright\\n\\nAll copyrights and related rights in this work are waived under CC0 1.0 Universal.", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-47.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "Proposal to distribute and decentralize staking power across the Polygon chain", "summary": "Proposal to increase the minimum commission.", "description": "### Abstract:\\n\\nPolygon has seen an explosion in staking in the over one year mainnet has been active. All 100 validator slots are now filled, but the distribution of delegated MATIC is clustered around large validators with low or 0% commission. Unfortunately, this has caused centralization and puts the network at risk. By increasing the minimum commission, the validators, delegators, and the network can benefit:\\n\\n### Motivation:\\n\\nThe top 10 validators have received 82% of the total delegation. Out of those validators, only the Binance node has a commission greater than 4%, with 7 out of 10 having a 0% commission. These low commissions incentivize additional delegations, which further centralizes the network. 0% commission also removes incentive for validators to keep a high uptime, which further threatens network security.\\nIncreasing the minimum commission charged will give validators more incentive to utilize professional hardware and maintain the highest possible uptime. Increasing the minimum commission will also give delegators more nodes to choose from at the same reward level. This will certainly help to decentralize the network. A decentralized network is more secure, leading to safety of funds for both delegators and the Polygon network as a whole. Properly funding validators will allow nodes to invest in performant hardware, which will allow a greater uptime percentage. With greater uptime percentage, fewer checkpoints will be missed and that will translate into more block rewards for delegators.\\n\\n### Specification:\\n\\nCurrently, there is only a MAX_COMMISSION_RATE set to 100 in the Matic Stake Manager 7 contract. A new contract would be required to include a MIN_COMMISSION_RATE, possibly as a configurable variable for easy update in the future. In the below code, this MIN_COMMISSION_RATE is created, along with the code enforcing the new minimum.\\n\\n```\\nuint256 constant MIN_COMMISSION_RATE = 5;\\n\\nuint256 constant MAX_COMMISSION_RATE = 100;\\n\\n...\\n\\nfunction updateCommissionRate(uint256 validatorId, uint256 newCommissionRate) external {\\n\\n...\\n\\nrequire(newCommissionRate >= MIN_COMMISSION_RATE, \\"Incorrect value\\");\\n\\nrequire(newCommissionRate <= MAX_COMMISSION_RATE, \\"Incorrect value\\");\\n\\nvalidators[validatorId].commissionRate = newCommissionRate;\\n\\n}\\n\\n```\\n\\nA minimum of a 5% commission was selected as enough room for the average validator to maintain their servers, but not too much that it starts to cut into delegators rewards. This is also in line with many other PoS blockchains, where 0% commission is not as common. Tezos’ bakers average 8-15%, Harmony One validators average 2.5-10%, Persistence One validators average 5-10%, and Solana validators average 10%, to name a few.\\n\\n### Rationale:\\n\\n**A common misconception about the Polygon network is that staking rewards are distributed when a validator creates a block or sends a checkpoint, and delegators will get more rewards by staking with larger validators. This isn’t completely accurate, as any validator that is online to validate a checkpoint will receive rewards. Rewards are sent to validators and delegators based on their total stake in the network. Therefore, outside of commission, the only variables in rewards are how much a delegator stake, and the uptime of the validator. Not only does staking with smaller validators provide the same rewards, it encourages better decentralization of the network.**\\n\\nOther options explored:\\n\\n- Putting a max cap on each validator’s total stake\\n- Decreasing rewards if a validator’s total stake surpasses a threshold\\n- Requiring a validator to stake a percentage of the total stake on their node, which incentivizes good behavior among larger validators\\n\\n### Backwards Compatibility:\\n\\nThere are no backwards compatibility issues in terms of functionality, as having a validator change their commission is already built in. However, the current contract would not be usable as there is no way to enforce a minimum commission, so a contract would need to be deployed.\\n\\n### **Security Considerations:**\\n\\nAlthough there is not much risk in implementing a minimum commission, any modification or new deployment of a contract inherits risks of bugs. Issues with the updateCommissionRate should only be limited to allowing validators to choose any number for their commission, and should not extend into other parts of the contract or Polygon ecosystem.\\n\\n---\\n\\n### References\\n\\n[Polygon Forum Discussion](https://forum.polygon.technology/t/pip-2-proposal-to-distribute-and-decentralize-staking-power-across-the-polygon-chain/219/1)", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-02.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "EIP-3074-style Account Abstraction", "summary": "Implementation of the `AUTH` and `AUTHCALL` EVM opcodes", "description": "## Abstract\\n\\nThis PIP proposes the implementation of two new EVM opcodes: `AUTH` and `AUTHCALL`, enabling account abstraction. This is not a novel proposal; this was proposed for Ethereum in [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074), which is as of yet not implemented on Ethereum.\\n\\n## Motivation\\n\\nAccount Abstraction is the name given to a number of proposals and tools intended to extend the functionality of \\"accounts\\" in Ethereum, whether contracts or EOAs. [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) was proposed as an avenue to increase the power of EOAs (by using heavily audited and vetted entry point contracts traditionally called invokers) by enabling the ability at the protocol level for one EOA to authorize another address to spend its native asset. This unlocks a number of functionalities, and also stands to potentially enhance UX. Advanced use cases built on this concept include session keys for games and allowing gas payment in ERC20 tokens, both without needing the standard two transaction approve-and-transfer mechanism which currently dominates the ecosystem with ERC20 tokens.\\n\\nWhile [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) has not been implemented on Ethereum as of this writing, it is the author's opinion that this does not reflect poorly on the EIP/PIP. EIP-3074 has been developed significantly, primarily by Quilt, and included repositories of patched versions of Solidity and the Geth client. It is also the author's belief that this is an excellent opportunity for Ethereum-aligned ecosystems to serve as the proving ground for proposals that can aid Ethereum.\\n\\n**_Note_:** The Specification and other sections of the PIP lean heavily on, or are outright copied from, [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074), written by Ansgar Dietrichs, Sam Wilson, lightclients, and Micah Zoltu. The authors have been contacted and have given their permission, and the work is also copyright-waived via [CC0](https://eips.ethereum.org/LICENSE).\\n\\n## Specification\\n\\n_copied from [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) with permission from the authors_\\n\\n### Conventions\\n\\n - **`top - N`** - the `N`th most recently pushed value on the EVM stack, where `top - 0` is the most recent.\\n - **`||`** - byte concatenation operator.\\n - **invalid execution** - execution that is invalid and must exit the current execution frame immediately, consuming all remaining gas (in the same way as a stack underflow or invalid jump).\\n\\n### Constants\\n\\n|     Constant     | Value  |\\n| ---------------- | ------ |\\n| `MAGIC`          | `0x04` |\\n\\n`MAGIC` is used for [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) signatures to prevent signature collisions with other signing formats.\\n\\n### Context Variables\\n\\n|     Variable        | Type      | Initial Value |\\n| ------------------- | --------- |:------------- |\\n| `authorized`        | `address` | unset         |\\n\\nThe context variable `authorized` shall indicate the active account for `AUTHCALL` instructions in the current frame of execution. If set, `authorized` shall only contain an account which has given the contract authorization to act on its behalf. An unset value shall indicate that no such account is set and that there is not yet an active account for `AUTHCALL` instructions in the current frame of execution.\\n\\nThe variable has the same scope as the program counter -- `authorized` persists throughout a single frame of execution of the contract, but is not passed through any calls (including `DELEGATECALL`). If the same contract is being executed in separate execution frames (ex. a `CALL` to self), both frames shall have independent values for `authorized`. Initially in each frame of execution, `authorized` is always unset, even if a previous execution frame for the same contract has a value.\\n\\n### `AUTH` (`0xf6`)\\n\\nA new opcode `AUTH` shall be created at `0xf6`. It shall take three stack element inputs (the last two describing a memory range), and it shall return one stack element.\\n\\n#### Input\\n\\n##### Stack\\n\\n| Stack      | Value        |\\n| ---------- | ------------ |\\n| `top - 0`  | `authority`  |\\n| `top - 1`  | `offset`     |\\n| `top - 2`  | `length`     |\\n\\n##### Memory\\n\\nThe final two stack arguments (`offset` and `length`) describe a range of memory. The format of the contents of that range is:\\n\\n - `memory[offset    : offset+1 ]` - `yParity`\\n - `memory[offset+1  : offset+33]` - `r`\\n - `memory[offset+33 : offset+65]` - `s`\\n - `memory[offset+65 : offset+97]` - `commit`\\n\\n#### Output\\n\\n##### Stack\\n\\n| Stack      | Value        |\\n| ---------- | -------------|\\n| `top - 0`  | `success`    |\\n\\n##### Memory\\n\\nMemory is not modified by this instruction.\\n\\n#### Behavior\\n\\nIf `length` is greater than 97, the extra bytes are ignored for signature verification (they still incur a gas cost as defined later). Bytes outside the range (in the event `length` is less than 97) are treated as if they had been zeroes.\\n\\n`authority` is the address of the account which generated the signature. If `EXTCODESIZE` of `authority` is not zero, consider the operation unsuccessful and unset `authorized`.\\n\\nThe arguments (`yParity`, `r`, `s`) are interpreted as an ECDSA signature on the secp256k1 curve over the message `keccak256(MAGIC || chainId || nonce || invokerAddress || commit)`, where:\\n\\n - `chainId` is the current chain's [EIP-155](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-155.md) unique identifier padded to 32 bytes.\\n - `nonce` is the signer's current nonce, left-padded to 32 bytes. Any other value is considered invalid.\\n - `invokerAddress` is the address of the contract executing `AUTH` (or the active state address in the context of `CALLCODE` or `DELEGATECALL`), left-padded with zeroes to a total of 32 bytes (ex. `0x000000000000000000000000AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA`).\\n - `commit`, one of the arguments passed into `AUTH`, is a 32-byte value that can be used to commit to specific additional validity conditions in the invoker's pre-processing logic.\\n\\nSignature validity and signer recovery are handled analogously to transaction signatures, including the stricter `s` range for preventing ECDSA malleability. Note that `yParity` is expected to be `0` or `1`.\\n\\nIf the signature is valid and the signer address is equal to `authority`, the context variable `authorized` is set to the `authority`. In particular, this is also true if `authority == tx.origin`, which used to be handled separately in earlier versions of this EIP (see Security Considerations). If the signature is instead invalid or the signer address does not equal `authority`, `authorized` is reset to an unset value.\\n\\n`AUTH` returns `1` if `authorized` is set, or `0` otherwise.\\n\\n#### Gas Cost\\n\\nThe gas cost for `AUTH` is equal to the sum of:\\n\\n - fixed fee `3100`.\\n - memory expansion gas cost (`auth_memory_expansion_fee`).\\n - `100` if `authority` is warm, `2600` if it is cold (per [EIP-2929](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-2929.md)).\\n\\nThe fixed fee is equal to the cost for the `ecrecover` precompile, plus a bit extra to cover a keccak256 hash and some additional logic.\\n\\nThe memory expansion gas cost (`auth_memory_expansion_fee`) shall be calculated in the same way as `RETURN`, where memory is expanded if the specified range is outside the current allocation.\\n\\n### `AUTHCALL` (`0xf7`)\\n\\nA new opcode `AUTHCALL` shall be created at `0xf7`. It shall take seven stack elements and return one stack element. It matches the behavior of the existing `CALL` (`0xF1`) instruction, except where noted below.\\n\\n#### Input\\n\\n| Stack     | Value        |\\n| --------- | ------------ |\\n| `top - 0` | `gas`        |\\n| `top - 1` | `addr`       |\\n| `top - 2` | `value`      |\\n| `top - 3` | `argsOffset` |\\n| `top - 4` | `argsLength` |\\n| `top - 5` | `retOffset`  |\\n| `top - 6` | `retLength`  |\\n\\n#### Output\\n\\n| Stack      | Value     |\\n| ---------- | --------- |\\n| `top - 0`  | `success` |\\n\\n#### Behavior\\n\\n`AUTHCALL` is interpreted the same as `CALL`, except for (note: this list is also the order of precedence for the logical checks):\\n\\n - If `authorized` is unset, execution is invalid (as defined above). Otherwise, the caller address for the call is set to `authorized`.\\n - The gas cost, including how much gas is available for the subcall, is specified in the Gas Cost section.\\n - If the `gas` operand is equal to `0`, the instruction will send all available gas as per [EIP-150](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-150.md).\\n - If the gas available for the subcall would be less than `gas`, execution is invalid.\\n - There is no gas stipend, even for non-zero `value`.\\n - `value` is deducted from the balance of `authorized`. If `value` is higher than the balance of `authorized`, execution is invalid.\\n\\n`AUTHCALL` must increase the call depth by one. `AUTHCALL` must not increase the call depth by two as it would if it first called into the authorized account and then into the target.\\n\\nThe return data area accessed with `RETURNDATASIZE` (`0x3d`) and `RETURNDATACOPY` (`0x3e`) must be set in the same way as the `CALL` instruction.\\n\\nImportantly, `AUTHCALL` does not reset `authorized`, but leaves it unchanged.\\n\\n#### Gas Cost\\n\\nThe gas cost for `AUTHCALL` shall be the **sum** of:\\n\\n - static gas cost (`warm_storage_read`)\\n - memory expansion gas cost (`memory_expansion_fee`)\\n - dynamic gas cost (`dynamic_gas`)\\n - gas available for execution in the subcall (`subcall_gas`)\\n\\nThe memory expansion gas cost (`memory_expansion_fee`) shall be calculated in the same way as `CALL`.\\n\\nThe dynamic gas portion (`dynamic_gas`), and the gas available for execution in the subcall (`subcall_gas`) shall be calculated as:\\n\\n```\\ndynamic_gas = 0\\n\\nif addr not in accessed_addresses:\\n    dynamic_gas += 2500         # cold_account_access - warm_storage_read\\n\\nif value > 0:\\n    dynamic_gas += 6700         # NB: Not 9000, like in `CALL`\\n    if is_empty(addr):\\n        dynamic_gas += 25000\\n\\nremaining_gas = available_gas - dynamic_gas\\nall_but_one_64th = remaining_gas - (remaining_gas // 64)\\n\\nif gas == 0:\\n    subcall_gas = all_but_one_64th\\nelif all_but_one_64th < gas:\\n    raise                       # Execution is invalid.\\nelse:\\n    subcall_gas = gas\\n```\\n\\nAs with `CALL`, the full gas cost is charged immediately, independently of actually executing the call.\\n\\n## Rationale\\n\\n_copied from [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) with permission from the authors_\\n\\n### Signature in Memory\\n\\nThe signature format (`yParity`, `r`, and `s`) is fixed, so it might seem curious that `auth` accepts a dynamic memory range. The signature is placed in memory so that `auth` can be upgraded in the future to work with contract accounts (which might use non-ECDSA signatures) and not just EOAs.\\n\\n### Signing Address `auth` Argument\\n\\nIncluding `authority` (the signing address) as an argument to `auth` allows future upgrades to the instruction to work with contract accounts, and not just EOAs.\\n\\nIf `authority` were not included and multiple signature schemes allowed, it would not be possible to compute the authorizing account's address from just the signature alone.\\n\\n### Reserving One Sixty-Fourth of Available Gas\\n\\n`AUTHCALL` will not pass more than 63/64th of the available gas for the reasons enumerated in [EIP-150](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-150.md).\\n\\n### Throwing for Unset `authorized` During `AUTHCALL`\\n\\nA well-behaved contract should never reach an `AUTHCALL` without having successfully set `authorized` beforehand. The safest behavior, therefore, is to exit the current frame of execution immediately. This is especially important in the context of transaction sponsoring / relaying, which is expected to be one of the main use cases for this EIP. In a sponsored transaction, the inability to distinguish between a sponsee-attributable fault (like a failing sub-call) and a sponsor-attributable fault (like a failing `AUTH`) is especially dangerous and should be prevented because it charges unfair fees to the sponsee.\\n\\n### Another Sponsored Transaction EIP\\n\\nThere are two general approaches to separating the \\"fee payer\\" from the \\"action originator\\".\\n\\nThe first is introducing a new transaction type. This requires significant changes to clients to support and is generally less upgradeable than other solutions (e.g. this EIP). This approach is also not immediately compatible with account abstraction (AA). These proposals require a *signed* transaction from the sponsor's account, which is not possible from an AA contract, because it has no private key to sign with. The main advantage of new transaction types is that the validity requirements are enforced by the protocol, therefore invalid transactions do not pollute block space.\\n\\nThe other main approach is to introduce a new mechanism in the EVM to masquerade as other accounts. This EIP introduces `AUTH` and `AUTHCALL` to make calls as EOAs. There are many different permutations of this mechanism. An alternative mechanism would be to add an opcode that can make arbitrary calls based on a similar address creation scheme as `CREATE2`. Although this mechanism would not benefit users today, it would immediately allow for those accounts to send and receive ether -- making it feel like a more first-class primitive.\\n\\nBesides better compatibility with AA, introducing a new mechanism into the EVM is a much less intrusive change than a new transaction type. This approach requires no changes in existing wallets, and little change in other tooling.\\n\\n`AUTHCALL`'s single deviation from `CALL` is to set `CALLER`. It implements the minimal functionality to enable sender abstraction for sponsored transactions. This single mindedness makes `AUTHCALL` significantly more composable with existing Ethereum features.\\n\\nMore logic can be implemented around the `AUTHCALL` instruction, giving more control to invokers and sponsors without sacrificing security or user experience for sponsees.\\n\\n### What to Sign?\\n\\nAs originally written, this proposal specified a precompile with storage to track nonces. Since a precompile with storage is unprecedented, a revision moved replay protection into the invoker contract, necessitating a certain level of user trust in the invoker. Expanding on this idea of trusted invokers, the other signed fields were eventually eliminated, one by one, until only `invoker` and `commit` remained. To appease concerns about cross-chain replay attacks and irrevocable signatures, the `chainId` and `nonce` fields returned to the signed message.\\n\\nThe `invoker` binds a particular signed message to a single invoker. If invoker was not part of the message, any invoker could reuse the signature to completely compromise the EOA. This allows users to trust that their message will be validated as they expect, particularly the values committed to in `commit`.\\n\\n### Understanding `commit`\\n\\nEarlier iterations of this EIP included mechanisms for replay protection, and also signed over value, gas, and other arguments to `AUTHCALL`. After further investigation, we revised this EIP to its current state: explicitly delegate these responsibilities to the invoker contract.\\n\\nA user will specifically interact with an invoker they trust. Because they trust this contract to execute faithfully, they will \\"commit\\" to certain properties of a call they would like to make by computing a hash of the call values. They can be certain that the invoker will only allow the call to proceed if it is able to verify the values committed to (e.g. a nonce to protect against replay attacks). This certainty arises from the `commit` value that is signed over by the user. This is the hash of values which the invoker will validate. A safe invoker should accept the values from the user and compute the commit hash itself. This ensures that invoker operated on the same input that user authorized.\\n\\n![auth message format](../assets/PIP-22/auth-msg.png)\\n\\nUsing `commit` as a hash of values allows for invokers to implement arbitrary constraints. For example, they could allow accounts to have `N` parallel nonces. Or, they could allow a user to commit to multiple calls with a single signature. This would allow multi-tx flows, such as [ERC-20](https://github.com/ethereum/ercs/blob/master/ERCS/erc-20.md) `approve`-`transfer` actions, to be condensed into a single transaction with a single signature verification. A commitment to multiple calls would look something like the diagram below.\\n\\n![multi-call auth message](../assets/PIP-22/auth-msg-multi-call.png)\\n\\nAnother interesting use is to delegate control of the EOA to other key(s). This would mean the EOA signs a `commit` message with the address of the key(s) and an access policy, if applicable. When the delegate wants to make a call as the EOA it will construct a signature over the invoker-specified call format and relay it (with the actual call data) onto chain with the signature and commit that granted it access to the account. The invoker will then be able to determine that the EOA has allowed this alternative key to make calls on its behalf.\\n\\n![delegate auth message](../assets/PIP-22/auth-msg-delegate.png)\\n\\n\\n### Invoker Contracts\\n\\nThe invoker contract is a trustless intermediary between the sponsor and sponsee. A sponsee signs over `invoker` to require the transaction to be processed only by a contract they trust. This allows them to interact with sponsors without needing to trust them.\\n\\nChoosing an invoker is similar to choosing a smart contract wallet implementation. It's important to choose one that has been thoroughly reviewed, tested, and accepted by the community as secure. We expect a few invoker designs to be utilized by most major transaction relay providers, with a few outliers that offer more novel mechanisms.\\n\\nAn important note is that invoker contracts **MUST NOT** be upgradeable. If an invoker can be redeployed to the same address with different code, it would be possible to redeploy the invoker with code that does not properly verify `commit` and any account that signed a message over that invoker would be compromised. Although this sounds scary, it is no different than using a smart contract wallet via `DELEGATECALL`. If the wallet is redeployed with different logic, all wallets using its code could be compromised.\\n\\n### On Call Depth\\n\\nThe EVM limits the maximum number of nested calls, and naively allowing a sponsor to manipulate the call depth before reaching the invoker would introduce a griefing attack against the sponsee. That said, with the 63/64th gas rule, and the cost of `AUTHCALL`, the stack is effectively limited to a much smaller depth than the hard maximum by the `gas` parameter.\\n\\nIt is, therefore, sufficient for the invoker to guarantee a minimum amount of gas, because there is no way to reach the hard maximum call depth with any reasonable (i.e. less than billions) amount of gas.\\n\\n### Source of `value`\\n\\nIn previous iterations of this EIP, it was thought that deducting value from an EOA mid-execution was problematic. This was due to an invariant of pending transactions which allows tx pools to statically determine the validity of a given transaction.\\n\\nHowever, after further investigation we found that breaking the invariant is safe. This is mostly due to the fact that the worst case is similar in both instances.\\n\\nCurrently an attacker can queue many transactions in the tx pool, across many accounts, and invalidate them all at once with a block where each of the queued accounts send a tx moving their entire balance. This attack will become easier and cheaper after this EIP, because it will no longer require direct access to the block builder and will not cost a full 21000 gas to originate each tx. However, the attack does not have a substantial impact on the network, so reducing the difficulty and cost is not of concern.\\n\\n### Allowing `tx.origin` as Signer\\n\\nAllowing `authorized` to equal `tx.origin` enables simple transaction batching, where the sender of the outer transaction would be the signing account. The ERC-20 approve-then-transfer pattern, which currently requires two separate transactions, could be completed in a single transaction with this proposal.\\n\\n`AUTH` allows for signatures to be signed by `tx.origin`. For any such signatures, subsequent `AUTHCALL`s have `msg.sender == tx.origin` in their first layer of execution. Without EIP-3074, this situation can only ever arise in the topmost execution layer of a transaction. This EIP breaks that invariant and so affects smart contracts containing `require(msg.sender == tx.origin)` checks. This check can be used for at least three purposes:\\n\\n 1. Ensuring that `msg.sender` is an EOA (given that `tx.origin` always has to be an EOA). This invariant does not depend on the execution layer depth and, therefore, is not affected.\\n 2. Protecting against atomic sandwich attacks like flash loans, that rely on the ability to modify state before and after the execution of the target contract as part of the same atomic transaction. This protection would be broken by this EIP. However, relying on `tx.origin` in this way is considered bad practice, and can already be circumvented by miners conditionally including transactions in a block.\\n 3. Preventing reentrancy.\\n\\nExamples of (1) and (2) can be found in contracts deployed on Ethereum mainnet, with (1) being more common (and unaffected by this proposal.) On the other hand, use case (3) is more severely affected by this proposal, but the authors of this EIP did not find any examples of this form of reentrancy protection, though the search was non-exhaustive.\\n\\nThis distribution of occurrences—many (1), some (2), and no (3)—is exactly what the authors of this EIP expect, because:\\n\\n - Determining if `msg.sender` is an EOA without `tx.origin` is difficult (if not impossible.)\\n - The only execution context which is safe from atomic sandwich attacks is the topmost context, and `tx.origin == msg.sender` is the only way to detect that context.\\n - In contrast, there are many direct and flexible ways of preventing reentrancy (ex. using a storage variable.) Since `msg.sender == tx.origin` is only true in the topmost context, it would make an obscure tool for preventing reentrancy, rather than other more common approaches.\\n\\nThere are other approaches to mitigate this restriction which do not break the invariant:\\n\\n - Set `tx.origin` to a constant `ENTRY_POINT` address for `AUTHCALL`s.\\n - Set `tx.origin` to the invoker address for `AUTHCALL`s.\\n - Set `tx.origin` to a special address derived from any of the sender, invoker, and/or signer addresses.\\n - Disallow `authorized == tx.origin`. This would make the simple batching use cases impossible, but could be relaxed in the future.\\n\\n### `AUTHCALL` cheaper than `CALL` when sending value\\n\\nSending non-zero value with `CALL` increases its cost by 9,000. Of that, 6,700 covers the increased overhead of the balance transfer and 2,300 is used as a stipend into the subcall to seed its gas counter. `AUTHCALL` does not provide a stipend and thus only charges the base 6,700.\\n\\n### In-Protocol Revocation\\n\\nThis EIP has gone [back and forth](#what-to-sign) on how to deal with `AUTH` message revocation. Without revocation, this EIP is a supremely powerful and flexible primitive for developers. However, it does have risk for users who use insecure and/or actively malicious invokers.\\n\\nMuch of the risk is due to the new ability for users to batch many operations in a single transaction. It becomes easier for an account to be drained. This is a risk that will continue to grow, regardless of the adoption of this EIP, due to overwhelming desire for the feature and attempts to support it at the protocol level and at the app level.\\n\\nA new class of risk is introduced for insecure and buggy invokers. If an invoker has implemented replay protection, as per the authors' recommendation, this should substantially contain the blast radius. However, if the bug allows an adversary to circumvent the replay protection mechanism, it may give them full access to any EOA which has interacted with the vulnerable invoker.\\n\\nAlthough this is a truly catastrophic event which is not expected to be possible via reputable wallets, it is a serious consideration. Without in-protocol revocation, users have no way to remove their account from the vulnerable invoker.\\n\\nFor this reason, `AUTH` requires the `nonce` in the message to be equal to the signer's current nonce. This way, a single tx from the EOA will cause the nonce to increase, invalidating all outstanding authorizations.\\n\\n### Failing on `EXTCODESIZE` check\\n\\nIn [EIP-3607](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-3607.md), it was determined that the protocol should reject any transaction which originates from an account with code. Although this EIP focused on transaction origination, the authors of EIP-3074 feel the intention is clear: an account that has both code and a known private key should not be allowed to make arbitrary calls on behalf of said account. Therefore, the property is upheld in this EIP. For full rationale, please refer to [EIP-3607](https://github.com/ethereum/EIPs/blob/master/EIPS/eip-3607.md).\\n\\n## Backwards Compatibility\\n\\nAlthough this EIP poses no issues for backwards compatibility, there are concerns that it limits future changes to accounts by further enshrining ECDSA signatures. For example, it might be desirable to eradicate the concept of EOAs altogether, and replace them with smart contract wallets that emulate the same behavior. This is fully compatible with the EIP as written, however, it gets tricky if users can then elect to \\"upgrade\\" their smart contract wallets to use other methods of authentication -- e.g. convert into a multi-sig. Without any changes, `AUTH` would not respect this new logic and continue allowing the old private key to perform actions on behalf of the account.\\n\\nA solution to this would be at the same time that EOAs are removed, to modify the logic of `AUTH` to actually call into the account with some standard message and allow the account to determine if the signature / witness is valid. Further research should be done to understand how invokers would need to change in this situation and how best to write them in a future-compatible manner.\\n\\n## Reference Implementation\\n\\n* https://github.com/ethereum/go-ethereum/pull/28615, Geth (from original EIP authors)\\n\\n* https://github.com/maticnetwork/bor/pull/1226, Bor\\n\\nThis is a cherry-picked version of the original geth implementation (of which bor is a fork). It is still under testing and should be viewed as a work in progress.\\n\\n* https://github.com/Mrtenz/transaction-invoker, Mrtenz\\n\\nA sample invoker contract along with a patched version of Solidity. This version of Solidity also follows the outdated version of the EIP.\\n\\n* https://github.com/0xPolygon/account-abstraction-invoker, [Zero Ekkusu](https://github.com/ZeroEkkusu)\\n\\nReference for work on invokers for Polygon's Edge project based on Mrtenz's invoker, linked above. (3074 was not ultimately included in the Edge project, however.)\\n\\n## Security Considerations\\n\\n_copied from [EIP-3074](https://eips.ethereum.org/EIPS/eip-3074) with permission from the authors_\\n\\n### Secure Invokers\\n\\nThe following is a non-exhaustive list of checks/pitfalls/conditions that invokers *should* be wary of:\\n\\n - Replay protection (ex. a nonce) should be implemented by the invoker, and included in `commit`. Without it, a malicious actor can reuse a signature, repeating its effects.\\n - `value` should be included in `commit`. Without it, a malicious sponsor could cause unexpected effects in the callee.\\n - `gas` should be included in `commit`. Without it, a malicious sponsor could cause the callee to run out of gas and fail, griefing the sponsee.\\n - `addr` and `calldata` should be included in `commit`. Without them, a malicious actor may call arbitrary functions in arbitrary contracts.\\n\\nA poorly implemented invoker can *allow a malicious actor to take near complete control over a signer's EOA*.\\n\\n### Allowing `tx.origin` as Signer\\n\\nAllowing `authorized` to equal `tx.origin` has the possibility to:\\n\\n - Break atomic sandwich protections which rely on `tx.origin`;\\n - Break reentrancy guards of the style `require(tx.origin == msg.sender)`.\\n\\nThe authors of this EIP believe the risks of allowing `authorized` to equal `tx.origin` are acceptable for the reasons outlined in the Rationale section.\\n\\n### Sponsored Transaction Relayers\\n\\nIt is possible for the `authorized` account to cause sponsored transaction relayers to spend gas without being reimbursed by either invalidating the authorization (i.e. increasing the account's nonce) or by sweeping the relevant assets out of the account. Relayers should be designed with these cases in mind, possibly by requiring a bond to be deposited or by implementing a reputation system.\\n\\n## Copyright\\n\\nAll copyrights and related rights in this work are waived under [CC0 1.0 Universal](https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/legalcode).", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Github\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://github.com/maticnetwork/Polygon-Improvement-Proposals/blob/main/PIPs/PIP-22.md\\"}]"}

{"title": "Financial Incentive - Phoenix Day Event (YWAM Staff)", "summary": "We are submitting this proposal to request a financial incentive for the hours volunteered by Youth With A Mission (YWAM) staff, helping lead aspects of our recent Phoenix Day Event. Five of our staff members volunteered from 9:00 AM to 4:00 PM, totaling 7 hours each, and one additional staff member volunteered from 12:00 PM to 4:00 PM, totaling 4 hours. This amounts to a combined total of 39 hours worked. At a rate of $25 per hour, the total payment requested is $975. {"title": "GOV-001: Core Circle Governance", "summary": "Core circle governance agreement", "description": "

Article 1: Definitions and Interpretations

  • Core Membership : An individual is considered a Core Member if XX

  • Sociocratic Approach : A decision-making method emphasizing equivalence, transparency, and effectiveness through rounds of discussion aiming for consent.

  • Voting Fallback : A mechanism invoked when consensus cannot be reached through sociocratic methods, resorting to a formal vote.

Article 2: Membership & Voting Power

  • Membership Criteria : As defined in Article 1 (Core Membership).

  • Application Process :

    • Interested individuals submit their application to the Core Circle by October 1st of each year.

    • Existing Core Members review applications, with deliberation and voting (if necessary) concluding by December 1st.

  • Voting Structure : One Core Member, one vote.

Article 3: Decision-Making Process

  • Primary Method : Sociocratic Approach aiming for consensus.

  • Voting Fallback :

    • Initiated when consensus is unachievable after a reasonable number of sociocratic rounds.

    • Follows the Voting Requirements outlined in Article 5.

Article 4: Proposal Submission and Voting Schedule

  • Proposal Submission Window : Open at all times, with a deadline for annual review proposals set as October 1st (1.10).

  • Voting Period :

    • Commences immediately after proposal submission, if submitted outside the annual review cycle.

    • For annual reviews, voting concludes by December 1st (1.12).

Article 5: Alignment Cycle and Special Voting Arrangements

  • Annual Alignment Cycle (AAC) :

    • Held every November.

    • Week 1 : Focus on community bonding and alignment exercises.

    • Weeks 2 & 3 : Two 4-hour sessions each week for in-depth discussions and sociocratic decision-making processes.

    • Week 4 : Integration phase, concluding with a final call for voting by December 1st (1.12).

  • Voting Requirements for Decisions :

    • Quorum : 90% of registered Core Members must participate.

    • Approval Threshold : 90% of cast votes must be in favor to pass a proposal.

    • Exception for Unanimity : Immediate approval granted if both quorum and affirmative votes reach 100%.

Article 6: Governance of This Agreement

  • Amendments :

    • Proposed amendments follow the decision-making process outlined in Article 3.

    • Must meet the Voting Requirements specified in Article 5 to be ratified.

  • Dispute Resolution :

    • Disputes regarding this agreement's interpretation or implementation are resolved through the sociocratic approach.

    • If unresolved, may invoke the Voting Fallback mechanism.

Article 7: Acknowledgement and Acceptance

By participating in the Core Circle as a recognized Core Member, individuals acknowledge that they have read, understand, and agree to be bound by this Core Circle Governance Agreement.

New Member Onboarding: New members will be required to sign an acknowledgement of acceptance as part of their onboarding process.

{"title": "NODEIFI DAO Membership Bylaws & Governance Framework", "summary": "This document establishes the NODEIFI DAO Membership Attestation and Governance Bylaws, outlining membership criteria, responsibilities, governance structure, decision-making processes, treasury oversight, and the future transition plan.", "description": "

NODEIFI DAO Membership Bylaws

NODEIFI DAO Membership Attestation

Purpose & Scope

This attestation serves as a formal declaration of membership within NODEIFI DAO. By signing or acknowledging this document, members affirm their commitment to the principles, governance structure, and operational guidelines established by the DAO.

Membership Criteria

To qualify as a NODEIFI DAO member, individuals or entities must meet the following criteria:

  • Possess an approved wallet address designated as a DAO member wallet.

  • Adhere to the governance principles and rules outlined in the DAO’s interim governance bylaws.

  • Actively participate in DAO activities, governance discussions, and voting processes.

  • Conduct themselves in alignment with the DAO’s ethical and operational standards.

Responsibilities & Expectations

As a member of NODEIFI DAO, individuals agree to:

  • Uphold the principles of decentralization, transparency, and collective decision-making.

  • Act in good faith to support the best interests of the DAO and its ecosystem.

  • Participate in governance decisions, including proposal reviews and voting.

  • Comply with all interim governance bylaws and any amendments ratified by the DAO.

  • Avoid conflicts of interest and disclose any potential conflicts when engaging in DAO matters.

Declaration of Purpose & Interim Governance Bylaws

Purpose & Vision

NODEIFI DAO is established to advance the Velocity Foundation ecosystem by fostering innovation, decentralisation, and strategic growth. The DAO exists to provide governance, treasury oversight, and community-driven decision-making that aligns with its long-term objectives.

Governance Framework

The governance of NODEIFI DAO is structured around:

  • Executive Team: Responsible for the execution of operational and strategic initiatives.

  • DAO Members: Participate in voting and governance discussions.

  • Proposals & Voting: Decisions are made collectively via proposals submitted and voted upon by members.

Executive Team Responsibilities

(Refer to the NODEIFI DAO Executive Team document for specific roles and responsibilities.)

Decision-Making Process

  • Proposals must be submitted through the DAO’s governance platform and adhere to the standard review process.

  • Voting mechanisms will be determined based on membership structure (initially wallet-based, with future potential for NFTs or tokens).

  • A proposal is considered approved upon meeting the required quorum and majority threshold.

Treasury Oversight

  • Treasury management will be conducted with transparency and strategic intent.

  • Major financial allocations must be reviewed by the Executive Team and ratified via DAO vote.

Disciplinary & Dismissal Procedures

  • Members or Executive Team members found in violation of governance principles may face disciplinary action, including warnings, suspension, or removal.

  • Any disciplinary action must follow a structured review process to ensure fairness and adherence to governance standards.

Future Transition Plan

  • As NODEIFI DAO evolves, governance mechanisms may shift towards token-based or NFT-based models.

  • Bylaws will be reviewed periodically to reflect changes in DAO structure, governance requirements, and ecosystem growth.

By participating in NODEIFI DAO, members agree to abide by these bylaws and contribute to the sustainable growth of the DAO.

In the future

Next phase is mostly about integrating bluetooth as a communication method between agents. Bluetooth is preferable because it allows for message exchange, including between unfamiliar devices, without the need to configure a Wi-Fi network. This provides a modest speed, but it is sufficient for exchanging meta-information and, if necessary, for organizing a separate high-speed communication channel.

Case that shows milestone completed: Laptop can connect to pi via bluetooth, transfer agent's libp2p message with a config and connect via terminal to simple docker container. Crate should also be available to use without agent.

Due to the need to be on external job, milestone timeline are extended.

Milestone Grant expenses

Requested amount: $4000

Estimated time

Milestone is planned from 25 of December 2024 to 28 of Febraury

{"title": "Grant Proposal: Decentralized utilities for robotics engineers", "summary": "Robot agent based on libp2p allows for easier and more flexible message sharing across different communication methods. By using libp2p's system for keeping track of device addresses, devices can be recognized without depending on the specific transport method being used. This simplifies the development process and improves compatibility between various robotic systems. As a result, it makes the design of robotic applications simpler and supports a stronger, more adaptable ecosystem. To be complementary to an existing system, we propose to develop rust bluetooth libp2p transport and integrate it with a basis robot agent.\\n\\nAgent development will simplify the integration of new devices with the robonomics network due to the addition of libp2p node functionality and identity keypair per device. It is also expected to monetize the solution through consulting and IaaS services with DAOs and related companies. Launched agents can directly communicate with the robonomics network increasing the number of useful transactions to the network.", "description": "

Estimated time may be changed during development robot-agent: data nodes (1 week)
robot-agent: basic web interface(list of robots/jobs/statuses) (1 week)
libp2p bluetooth: first prototype - identify and sending of basic messages (2 weeks)
blog post: about libp2 bluetooth messaging
libp2p: enhance number of messages types (1 week)
libp2p bluetooth: work on stability and DX(helpers to setup bluetooth)(1 week)
blog post: how libp2 helps robotics engineers

robot-agent: messages encryption (0.5 week)
libp2p bluetooth: basic benchmarking(1 week)
robot-agent: access logs and data from devices via web app (1 week) robot-agent: overlay network setup (2 weeks)
blog post: How we set up robotics fleet VPN on fly

robot-agent: module to configure network on device (1 week)
robot-agent: module to install packages/apps on device (1 week)
robot-agent: web app should provide same functionality as cli (1 week)
libp2p bluetooth: testing and bug fixes (1 week)
libp2p bluetooth: benchmarking and report(1 week)
*blog post: final about libp2p bluetooth

", "resourcesJson": "[{\\"name\\":\\"Gist\\",\\"url\\":\\"https://gist.github.com/Smehnov/388f62208b02dbd6a50dfc1d1a57a1d8\\"}]"}

{"title": "First milestone for Decentralized utilities for robotics engineers", "summary": "Robotics engineers understand the challenges of managing a fleet of devices, as current solutions are often inconvenient or reliant on proprietary software. Current messaging systems (for example in ROS 2) have limitations because they depend on specific transport protocols, which makes it hard to scale and work with other systems. As robotic systems use more different communication technologies, there is a strong need for a solution that allows for consistent message sharing without needing a lot of reconfiguration. This reliance on certain protocols creates fragmentation, making system design more complicated and slowing down collaboration across different platforms.", "description": "
